originalClaim: The matrix assumes a universal consent-age floor of 13 (consent_age_floor=13) for all 10 jurisdictions (US, UK, NO, DE, FR, SK, SE, DK, EE, GR), treated as the lowest defensible self-consent threshold.

verify.correction: The claim is correct for 9 of 10 jurisdictions. Denmark (DK) is wrong: Law No. 1783 of 28/12/2023 raised Denmark's digital consent age floor from 13 to 15 in databeskyttelsesloven §6(2), effective 1 January 2024 (consolidated as LBK No. 289 of 8 March 2024). DK's consent_age_floor and consent_age_default in the matrix should both be 15, not 13. Secondary tracker sites (including the EDPB tracker cited as the matrix source) still show the stale 13 value — exactly the stale-tracker failure mode the trusted prior findings flagged. The matrix's DK label ("DK derogated to 13 — EDPB tracker secondary") is an unverified secondary citation that is now outdated.
verify.notes: The matrix's use of consent_age_floor=13 as "lowest defensible self-consent threshold" is a reasonable framing for the other 9 jurisdictions (it captures the GDPR Art 8 minimum floor, with consent_age_default capturing each state's actual operative age). But for DK this framing fails entirely: the operative floor is now 15, and there is no longer any derogation down to 13 in Danish law. The DK cell is wrong in both the floor and the default fields.

The matrix separately carries each state's actual operative age in consent_age_default (DE=16, FR=15, SK=16, GR=15, SE=13; EE='?'). The verifier's DK correction is directionally right but over-broad: only DK's consent_age_default is stale (should be 15, per LOV nr 1783 of 28/12/2023, eff. 2024-01-01) — DK's consent_age_floor=13 is CORRECT and should not change, because the floor field denotes the Art 8 minimum, not the operative age. Additionally, Norway's consent_age_default=13 is presently correct but under an active raise-to-15 proposal (Oct-2024, still in consultation), worth monitoring.
challenge.reasoning: The claim is purely about the consent_age_floor field being uniformly 13. The data file and README confirm this is the matrix's intentional design: floor=13 is the GDPR Art 8 minimum (the literal "lowest defensible self-consent threshold"), with operative ages held separately in consent_age_default. So the claim holds for all 10. The verifier's DK fix is right on the default but over-corrected the floor — 13 is correct for DK's floor. Norway's default-13 is correct now but fragile (active raise proposal). Net: claim sustained; verifier correction needs the floor-vs-default caveat.

originalClaim: In the United States, COPPA (16 CFR Part 312) sets the children's data-protection threshold at under 13, and is the consent-age default for the US.

The cited source (law.cornell.edu / 16 CFR Part 312) is correct. The 0.85 file confidence is conservative — primary law is unambiguous; 0.99 is appropriate. No post-2024 development changes the threshold.

evidence.source: 16 CFR § 312.2 — Cornell LII (primary statute text)
evidence.finding: "Child means an individual under the age of 13." Direct statutory text; the 2025 amendments (effective June 23, 2025; compliance deadline April 22, 2026) did not alter this definition.

originalClaim: The UK sets its digital-consent age at 13 via DPA 2018 section 123 plus retained UK GDPR (post-Brexit Art 8).

verify.correction: The UK digital consent age of 13 for ISS is correct and is anchored in retained UK GDPR Art 8. However, DPA 2018 section 123 is the wrong citation — it is the Children's Code (Age-Appropriate Design Code) enabling provision, not a consent-age provision. The companion DPA 2018 provision that once co-set the threshold (s.9, 'Child's consent in relation to ISS') was omitted on 31 December 2020 by Brexit-related regulations. Post-Brexit, the 13-year threshold flows from UK GDPR Art 8 alone. The matrix should cite: retained UK GDPR Art 8 (primary basis); DPA 2018 s.123 is relevant only as the Children's Code anchor, not as a consent-age provision.
verify.notes: The age (13) and the UK GDPR Art 8 citation are both correct. The DPA 2018 §123 citation is wrong — it is the Children's Code design-standards provision. The former consent-age companion (DPA 2018 s.9) was deleted post-Brexit. Matrix should correct the section reference and note that post-Brexit the sole statutory basis is UK GDPR Art 8.

evidence.source: legislation.gov.uk — UK GDPR Art 8 (retained EU law)
evidence.finding: UK GDPR Art 8 sets 13 as the minimum age for children to consent to ISS data processing independently; under-13s require parental consent. Age can be adjusted by Secretary of State but not below 13 or above 16. This correctly anchors the 13-year threshold.

evidence.source: legislation.gov.uk — DPA 2018 s.123
evidence.finding: Section 123 of DPA 2018 is the Children's Code (Age-Appropriate Design Code) enabling provision: it mandates the ICO to prepare a code on design standards for services likely accessed by children. It does NOT set a digital consent age threshold.

evidence.source: legislation.gov.uk — DPA 2018 s.9 (omitted)

No live DPA 2018 section now co-sets the 13-year threshold.

evidence.source: ICO — Children and the UK GDPR (search result summary)
evidence.finding: ICO confirms the 13-year digital consent age under UK GDPR Art 8 for ISS. The ICO's Children's Code (statutory under s.123 DPA 2018) addresses design standards, not the consent-age threshold itself.

evidence.source: Trusted prior finding (verified 2026-06-04/05)
evidence.finding: Prior verification pass confirmed UK=13 (UK GDPR/DPA 2018, ISS consent). Age is correct. The section number mis-citation (§123 vs. the now-omitted §9) is the error; post-Brexit the threshold rests on UK GDPR Art 8 alone.

challenge.correctedStatement: The UK digital-consent age for information society services is 13. The statutory anchor is UK GDPR Art 8(1) as amended by the Data Protection, Privacy and Electronic Communications (Amendments etc) (EU Exit) Regulations 2019 (SI 2019/419), which hard-coded 13 onto the face of Art 8 (the original retained-EU text said 16) and simultaneously omitted DPA 2018 s.9, the former co-setting provision, effective 31 December 2020. DPA 2018 s.123 is the Children's Code (Age-Appropriate Design Code) enabling provision — NOT a consent-age provision; cite it only as the Children's Code anchor. As of 29 April 2026, Children's Wellbeing and Schools Act 2026 inserted Art 8 paras 2A/2B (Secretary-of-State power to adjust within 13–16 by affirmative-procedure regulations); baseline remains 13. The Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 (in force 19 June 2025) did not alter the age or s.123.

My attack adds two things the verifier missed: (1) precise provenance — SI 2019/419 wrote "13" onto Art 8's face (was 16), so "Art 8 alone" should read "Art 8 as amended"; (2) a live 2026 layer — Children's Wellbeing and Schools Act 2026 paras 2A/2B (SoS adjust power). Neither overturns; both refine. Original citation wrong but verifier already fixed it.

originalClaim: Norway sets its age of digital consent at 13 via Personopplysningsloven section 5 (Norway's GDPR Art 8 implementation); above 13 the child self-consents, below 13 parental consent is required.

verify.notes: The claim is accurate: §5 is the correct section, 13 is the correct age, and the self-consent/parental-consent split is correct (though §5 itself only sets the 13 threshold; the below-13 parental-consent requirement derives from GDPR Art. 8(1) which §5 incorporates by reference). One forward-looking caveat: Norway's government submitted a bill to Parliament in 2026 targeting age 16 for social media specifically — not yet enacted; §5 remains 13. Matrix should note the pending raise proposal and monitor its enactment.

This is Norway's direct implementation of GDPR Art. 8, setting the threshold at 13.

originalClaim: Germany applies the GDPR Art 8 default consent age of 16 because it did not derogate downward; 14-16 requires parental consent and below 14 requires parental consent plus reasonable efforts to verify.

verify.correction: The first two elements are correct: Germany applies the GDPR Art 8 default age of 16 and did not derogate downward. However, the claim incorrectly describes a two-tier structure for under-16 children. GDPR Art 8 (as Germany applies it, without derogation) has only ONE threshold at 16: children under 16 require parental consent, AND controllers must make reasonable efforts to verify that consent (Art 8(2)). There is no separate "14–16" tier requiring only parental consent and no separate "below 14" tier requiring parental consent plus verification — the verification obligation applies uniformly to all children under 16. The "below 14 / 14-16" framing would apply only to a Member State that derogated Art 8's age down to 14 (e.g., Austria, Italy), creating a real split: 14-16 can consent alone, and under 14 requires parental consent + verification. Germany, having kept the default of 16, has no such split.
verify.notes: The matrix may be conflating Germany's rule with the rule that applies in countries that DID derogate Art 8 to 14 (AT, BG, CY, IT, LT, ES per trusted findings). In those countries the two-tier structure the claim describes is accurate; in Germany it is not. The cited sources (gdpr-text.com, EDPB tracker) are legitimate for the 16-age point but do not support the below-14 sub-tier. Consider correcting the cell to remove the spurious 14-year split.

evidence.source: GDPR Art 8 full text (gdpr-text.com / gdpr-info.eu)
evidence.finding: Art 8 creates a SINGLE threshold at 16. Below 16: parental consent required. Art 8(2) 'reasonable efforts to verify' applies to ALL children under 16, not a sub-group under 14. No two-tier structure exists within Art 8 itself.

evidence.source: EuConsent GDPR digital-consent-age tracker (euconsent.eu)
evidence.finding: Germany's digital consent age is 16; children below 16 require parental consent. Explicitly confirms no two-tier system — no separate rules for under-14 vs 14-16 in Germany.

evidence.source: Trusted prior finding #1 (verified 2026-06-04/05 adversarial pass)
evidence.finding: DE=16 confirmed in trusted prior findings. Germany did not derogate downward from GDPR Art 8's default of 16. This part of the claim is correct.

evidence.source: Riscreen.de Art 8 GDPR analysis
evidence.finding: Germany kept the 16-year default; BDSG does not create a sub-16 age tier. The 'reasonable efforts to verify' duty in Art 8(2) applies whenever parental consent is needed (i.e., all under-16), not specifically for under-14.

evidence.source: GDPR-info.eu Art 8
evidence.finding: Member States may derogate Art 8's 16-year default DOWN to 13 minimum. Germany did not do so. There is no German legal basis for a separate 'below 14' sub-tier with distinct obligations beyond what Art 8(2) already imposes on all under-16 cases.

challenge.correctedStatement: Germany applies the GDPR Art 8 default consent age of 16 and did not derogate downward, so it has ONE threshold: a child under 16 cannot consent alone to information-society-service processing based on consent; parental consent is required, and Art 8(2) "reasonable efforts to verify" parental consent applies uniformly to ALL under-16s. Germany has NO "14-16" or "below-14" sub-tier — that two-tier structure (14+ consent alone; under-14 parental consent + verification) exists only in Member States that derogated Art 8 down to 14 (e.g., Austria BDSG §14(4), Italy). Note: Art 8's rigid-16 rule governs ISS offered to a child relying on consent; for non-ISS or non-consent bases, German doctrine applies an individual Einsichtsfähigkeit assessment — but for a consumer AI-tutoring app relying on consent, the rigid 16 controls.
challenge.reasoning: Attack confirms the verifier, not the claim. Primary Art 8 text shows verification applies uniformly to all under-16 with no internal sub-tier; the "14-16/below-14" structure belongs to derogate-to-14 states (Austria, Italy), not Germany. The original claim is materially wrong (garbled hybrid — even in derogating states 14-16 needs NO parental consent), so it is weakened; the verifier's corrected statement survives intact. One enhancement: Art 8's rigid 16 is ISS-and-consent-specific (non-ISS uses Einsichtsfähigkeit), which the verifier omitted but doesn't alter the result. No 2026 change to Germany's 16.

counterEvidence.source: GDPR Art 8(1)-(3) full text (gdpr-text.com)
counterEvidence.finding: Art 8(2) verification of parental consent applies uniformly to ALL children below 16. No two-tier (under-14 vs 14-16) bracket exists within Art 8's text. The claim's German sub-tier is fabricated.

counterEvidence.source: EuConsent / iubenda on Austria & Italy derogations
counterEvidence.finding: The 'under-14 vs 14-16' split belongs to derogate-to-14 states: Austria BDSG §14(4) and Italy let 14+ consent alone, under-14 needs parental consent + Art 8(2) verification. Germany kept 16, so no such split.

counterEvidence.source: dr-datenschutz / datenschutz-praxis (DE legal commentary)
counterEvidence.finding: Germany did not exercise the Art 8(4) option to lower below 16; the rigid 16 floor applies. Pre-GDPR DE used a 14-year capacity presumption, but that does NOT survive as an Art 8 sub-tier today.

counterEvidence.source: PPC.land on German DSK resolution 20 Nov 2025
counterEvidence.finding: Nov 2025 German DPA resolution proposes banning child consent for profiling/advertising — a strengthening proposal, NOT enacted, and does not change the 16 age threshold. No 2025-26 change to Germany's 16.

counterEvidence.source: datenschutz-praxis on Art 8 scope
counterEvidence.finding: Art 8's 16-year rule applies only to ISS (Dienste der Informationsgesellschaft); outside ISS German doctrine uses individual Einsichtsfähigkeit. The verifier did not surface this scope caveat, though it does not change the answer for a consent-based AI-tutoring app.

final.statement: Germany applies the GDPR Art 8 default consent age of 16 and did not derogate downward, so it has ONE threshold: a child under 16 cannot consent alone to information-society-service processing based on consent; parental consent is required, and Art 8(2) "reasonable efforts to verify" parental consent applies uniformly to ALL under-16s. Germany has NO "14-16" or "below-14" sub-tier — that two-tier structure (14+ consent alone; under-14 parental consent + verification) exists only in Member States that derogated Art 8 down to 14 (e.g., Austria BDSG §14(4), Italy). Note: Art 8's rigid-16 rule governs ISS offered to a child relying on consent; for non-ISS or non-consent bases, German doctrine applies an individual Einsichtsfähigkeit assessment — but for a consumer AI-tutoring app relying on consent, the rigid 16 controls.
final.reasoning: Attack confirms the verifier, not the claim. Primary Art 8 text shows verification applies uniformly to all under-16 with no internal sub-tier; the "14-16/below-14" structure belongs to derogate-to-14 states (Austria, Italy), not Germany. The original claim is materially wrong (garbled hybrid — even in derogating states 14-16 needs NO parental consent), so it is weakened; the verifier's corrected statement survives intact. One enhancement: Art 8's rigid 16 is ISS-and-consent-specific (non-ISS uses Einsichtsfähigkeit), which the verifier omitted but doesn't alter the result. No 2026 change to Germany's 16.

originalClaim: France set its GDPR Art 8 digital-consent age to 15 by national derogation (upward from the floor of 13); above 15 the child self-consents, 13-15 requires parental consent.

verify.notes: One nuance worth noting for the matrix: French law (Art 45) requires *joint* consent of child AND parent for under-15 — not purely parental consent alone. The child's own assent is mandatory; a parent cannot override the child's refusal, nor can the child override a parent's objection. The claim's shorthand "requires parental consent" is not wrong but understates this joint-consent structure. The GDPR Art 8 mechanism is correctly characterised as a Member State derogation option (floor=13, ceiling=16; France chose 15). No post-2024 changes to this threshold found.

evidence.source: Légifrance — Article 45, Loi n° 78-17 du 6 janvier 1978 (Loi Informatique et Libertés, consolidated)
evidence.finding: Article 45 expressly states: a minor may consent alone to data processing for direct information-society services from age 15; below 15, processing is lawful only if consent is given jointly by the minor and the holder(s) of parental authority. Primary French statutory source confirmed.

evidence.source: CNIL — Recommendation 4: Seek parental consent for children under 15
evidence.finding: CNIL confirms France's digital consent age is 15, implemented via Article 45 of the Data Protection Act. Under-15 requires joint consent of child and parent(s); above 15 the child self-consents. Only one parent's express consent is needed; the other's is presumed.

evidence.source: CNIL — Digital rights of children (overview page)
evidence.finding: CNIL affirms the 15-year threshold for independent digital consent in France, with dual (child + parent) consent required below 15. Consistent with Article 45 and the prior verified finding that FR=15.

evidence.source: GDPR Article 8(1) — eur-lex.europa.eu (GDPR text)
evidence.finding: GDPR Art 8(1) sets the default digital consent age at 16 but permits Member States to provide by law for a lower age, not below 13. France exercised this option, setting 15 — an upward choice from the 13 floor, downward from the 16 default.

evidence.source: Trusted prior verified finding (verified 2026-06-04/05)
evidence.finding: Prior 45-claim verification pass confirmed FR=15 as one of the verified jurisdiction entries. Current primary-source fetch of Article 45 Légifrance and CNIL corroborates this fully.

final.statement: France set its GDPR Art 8 digital-consent age to 15 by national derogation (upward from the floor of 13); above 15 the child self-consents, 13-15 requires parental consent.
final.reasoning: One nuance worth noting for the matrix: French law (Art 45) requires *joint* consent of child AND parent for under-15 — not purely parental consent alone. The child's own assent is mandatory; a parent cannot override the child's refusal, nor can the child override a parent's objection. The claim's shorthand "requires parental consent" is not wrong but understates this joint-consent structure. The GDPR Art 8 mechanism is correctly characterised as a Member State derogation option (floor=13, ceiling=16; France chose 15). No post-2024 changes to this threshold found.

originalClaim: Denmark derogated its GDPR Art 8 consent age down to 13; above 13 the child self-consents, below 13 parental consent is required.

verify.correction: Denmark's GDPR Art 8 digital consent age is **15**, not 13. LOV nr 1783 af 28/12/2023 amended Databeskyttelsesloven §6(2) and (3), raising the threshold from 13 to 15, effective 1 January 2024. Above 15 the child self-consents; below 15 parental/guardian consent is required. The claim was accurate before 2024 but has been superseded.
verify.notes: Many secondary trackers (EuConsent, DataGuidance, Datatilsynet's own English PDF) still show the stale "13" figure — the exact failure mode flagged in trusted prior findings. The consolidated Danish statute (LBK 289/2024) and the DPO Danmark analysis both confirm 15 as the operative threshold. The matrix cell, SOURCES.md citation, and any downstream app logic gating parental-consent flows at age 13 for Denmark must be updated to 15.

evidence.source: LOV nr 1783 af 28/12/2023 (retsinformation.dk) — primary Danish statute
evidence.finding: Titled 'Adjustment of the age limit for consent to processing of personal data in connection with the supply of information society services.' Amended Databeskyttelsesloven §6(2) and (3) — changed '13 years' to '15 years'. Effective 1 January 2024.

evidence.source: DPO Danmark — Ændringer i databeskyttelsesloven fra 2024
evidence.finding: Confirms §6(2) threshold raised from 13 to 15 effective 2024-01-01. Children under 15 need guardian consent; children 15+ self-consent. Transitional rule: consents given before 2024-01-01 remain valid until renewal.

evidence.source: LBK nr 289 af 08/03/2024 (retsinformation.dk) — consolidated Danish Data Protection Act
evidence.finding: Consolidated version of Databeskyttelsesloven post-amendment, incorporating the LOV 1783 change — confirms 15 is the operative threshold in the current consolidated statute text.

evidence.source: Themis.dk — Databeskyttelsesloven (Danish legal synopsis)
evidence.finding: Section 6(2) as amended reads 15 years; corroborates that the pre-2024 age of 13 was superseded by LOV 1783/2023.

evidence.source: EuConsent tracker (euconsent.eu) — secondary, last updated 2021
evidence.finding: Still shows Denmark at 13 — stale; confirms the trusted-prior-finding warning that tracker sites and English translations lag behind the live consolidated Danish statute. Not reliable for current state.

challenge.correctedStatement: Denmark's GDPR Art 8 digital-consent age for information-society-service processing is 15 (not 13): LOV nr 1783 af 28/12/2023 §1.1 amended Databeskyttelsesloven §6(2)-(3), replacing "13 år" with "15 år", effective 1 Jan 2024. Above 15 the child self-consents; below 15 the holder of parental authority must consent/approve. The original 13 was accurate pre-2024 only. (Separate: a proposed under-15 social-media-access ban announced Nov 2025 is a distinct regime, not the data-consent age, and does not raise it to 16.)
challenge.reasoning: Attack failed to dent the verifier. Danish-language primary statutory text (themis.dk synopsis quoting §6) directly confirms the 13→15 change via LOV 1783/2023 §1.1, effective 2024-01-01 — matching the trusted prior finding (DK=15) and the exact section/subsections the verifier cited. Checked for a later raise to 16: the Nov-2025 under-15 social-media ban is a separate regime keeping 15; June-2025 "15-16" press is a restatement of the 2023 proposal, not enactment. Scope fits a consumer AI-tutoring ISS app. Verifier's "outdated→15" stands fully.

counterEvidence.source: Themis.dk synopsis of Databeskyttelsesloven §6 (quoting consolidated statute text)
counterEvidence.finding: Operative text: '§6 stk.2 og 3 ændret ved §1.1 i lov nr. 1783 af 28.12.2023 fra 01.01.2024. I §6 stk.2 og 3 ændres "13 år" til "15 år".' Confirms verifier's 15, primary-anchored. Claim's 13 is stale, not wrong-in-form.

counterEvidence.source: Grakom / DPO Danmark (Danish legal commentary)
counterEvidence.finding: Rationale: 15 chosen over EU's suggested 16 to harmonise with Danish health-consent and criminal/sexual age of consent (also 15). Confirms the threshold is 15 for ISS consent; under-15 needs guardian consent.

counterEvidence.source: Fortune / PBS — Denmark under-15 social-media access ban (Nov 7, 2025)
counterEvidence.finding: A SEPARATE 2025 regime: proposed ban on social-media access under 15 (parental exception from 13), targeted 2026. Does NOT raise the GDPR Art 8 data-consent age to 16 — keeps 15. So verifier's 15 is not superseded by a later move.

counterEvidence.source: Digital Watch / Reuters (June 2025 restatement)
counterEvidence.finding: The '15 to 16' phrasing in 2025 press is a restatement of the original 2023 proposal range, not a NEW enactment above 15. Enacted figure remains 15 (LOV 1783/2023). No primary evidence of a 16 threshold for data consent.

verify.correction: Estonia's GDPR Art 8 derogation is NOT unknown — it has been settled law since 15 January 2019. Section 8(1) of Estonia's Personal Data Protection Act (Riigi Teataja, RT I, 04.01.2019, 11) sets digital consent age at 13, exercising the Art 8 derogation to the minimum. The matrix should set EE consent_age_default = 13, not "?", and cite: "PDPA §8(1) (RT I, 04.01.2019, 11; in force 15.01.2019)".
verify.notes: The claim accurately describes the matrix's internal state (cell shows "?"; corpus gap acknowledged). The matrix should be corrected: set EE consent_age_default=13, cite PDPA §8(1). No AKI (Andmekaitse Inspektsioon) eIDAS-specific guidance was needed; the derogation is in the primary PDPA statute itself.

evidence.source: Estonia Personal Data Protection Act §8(1), Riigi Teataja RT I, 04.01.2019, 11 (in force 15.01.2019)

originalClaim: COPPA applies only if the service is 'directed to children' OR the controller has actual knowledge that a user is under 13; absent either, there is no COPPA jurisdiction (though FTC §5 unfair/deceptive-practices authority still binds).

verify.notes: Claim is accurate. One terminological note: COPPA uses 'operator' (not 'controller', which is GDPR vocabulary); the claim's substitution of 'controller' is a cross-regime vocabulary slip but does not affect the legal test. The 2025 Rule amendments (trusted finding 4) are now partially in force (June 2025 effective; April 2026 compliance deadline) but did not change the jurisdictional trigger — the OR structure holds as of 2026-06-05.

evidence.source: 15 USC § 6502(a) — COPPA statute, Office of the Law Revision Counsel / LII
evidence.finding: Statute text is exactly disjunctive: unlawful for 'an operator of a website or online service directed to children, OR any operator that has actual knowledge that it is collecting personal information from a child.' Absent both, no COPPA unlawful-acts obligation attaches.

evidence.source: 16 CFR § 312.3 — COPPA Rule (as amended 2025), Cornell LII
evidence.finding: Regulatory text mirrors statute: compliance is triggered by 'directed to children' OR 'actual knowledge of collecting personal information from a child.' The 2025 amendments (effective June 23 2025) did not alter this OR-trigger structure.

evidence.source: 15 USC § 6502(c) — FTC enforcement / Section 5 linkage
evidence.finding: COPPA violations are treated as violations of a rule defining an unfair or deceptive act or practice under Section 57a(a)(1)(B). FTC retains independent § 5 authority over general-audience services — confirmed by statute text.

evidence.source: FTC — COPPA Rule: Not Just for Kids' Sites (business guidance)
evidence.finding: FTC guidance explicitly states COPPA also covers general-audience sites when operators have actual knowledge of collecting children's data. Absence of both triggers = no COPPA obligation, consistent with the claim.

evidence.source: FTC — COPPA Final Rule Amendments, Federal Register (2025-04-22)
evidence.finding: 2025 amendments (effective June 23 2025, compliance April 22 2026) added VPC methods and retention rules (trusted prior finding 4) but left the fundamental 'directed to children OR actual knowledge' jurisdictional trigger unchanged.

final.statement: COPPA applies only if the service is 'directed to children' OR the controller has actual knowledge that a user is under 13; absent either, there is no COPPA jurisdiction (though FTC §5 unfair/deceptive-practices authority still binds).
final.reasoning: Claim is accurate. One terminological note: COPPA uses 'operator' (not 'controller', which is GDPR vocabulary); the claim's substitution of 'controller' is a cross-regime vocabulary slip but does not affect the legal test. The 2025 Rule amendments (trusted finding 4) are now partially in force (June 2025 effective; April 2026 compliance deadline) but did not change the jurisdictional trigger — the OR structure holds as of 2026-06-05.

originalClaim: COPPA's actual-knowledge trigger is activated by a user disclosing DOB, grade-level, school type, or saying their age in in-app chat (e.g. 'I'm 10'); once triggered, Verifiable Parental Consent (VPC) is required before any personal-information collection.

verify.correction: The claim is correct on three of four triggers: DOB disclosure, grade-level questions, and school-type questions are explicitly confirmed in FTC guidance (the 'Not Just for Kids' Sites' resource) as actual-knowledge triggers. The VPC-before-collection requirement once triggered is also confirmed. However, the claim overstates the legal certainty of the fourth trigger — a user spontaneously stating their age in free-form chat (e.g., 'I'm 10'). The FTC's rule text and official FAQ frame actual knowledge as arising when an operator asks for or collects age-identifying information through registration or structured inputs; passive chat disclosure is a legally plausible but not explicitly enumerated trigger in 16 CFR 312 or the official FAQ. Enforcement actions and the general principle ('collecting information that establishes a visitor is under 13') support extending the rule to chat disclosures, but operators cannot treat the chat scenario as a clearly settled statutory rule on par with the three enumerated triggers.
verify.notes: The 2025 COPPA amendments (effective June 23, 2025; compliance deadline April 22, 2026) did not change the actual knowledge standard. The matrix should note that (a) grade-level and school-type are explicitly FTC-confirmed triggers; (b) in-chat age disclosure is a defensible but not explicitly codified extension; and (c) the 0.85 confidence in the file is slightly high given the chat-disclosure ambiguity — 0.80 would be more accurate for the claim as stated.

evidence.source: FTC COPPA guidance: 'Children's Online Privacy Protection Rule: Not Just for Kids' Sites'
evidence.finding: FTC explicitly states asking 'What grade are you in?' or 'What type of school do you go to?' constitutes actual knowledge triggers. DOB that reveals under-13 age also triggers actual knowledge. Confirmed for grade-level and school-type elements of the claim.

evidence.source: FTC COPPA FAQ — Complying with COPPA: Frequently Asked Questions (ftc.gov)
evidence.finding: An operator who asks for DOB on a registration page and receives an under-13 year has actual knowledge. Asking for or collecting information that 'establishes that a visitor is under 13' triggers COPPA compliance obligations — framed as operator-initiated inquiry, not passive chat disclosure.

evidence.source: 16 CFR 312 (eCFR current text) + Gibson Dunn 2025 COPPA amendments analysis
evidence.finding: VPC is required before collecting personal information from children once actual knowledge is established — confirmed. The 2025 amendments (effective June 23, 2025; compliance April 22, 2026) did not alter the actual knowledge standard itself.

evidence.source: FTC COPPA 2025 Final Rule — Latham & Watkins analysis
evidence.finding: 2025 amendments focus on VPC methods, retention limits, security, and mixed-audience site definitions — not on changing the actual knowledge trigger standard. Effective June 23, 2025.

evidence.source: FTC search result synthesis — COPPA FAQ and 'Not Just for Kids' Sites' guidance
evidence.finding: The FTC's formal guidance enumerates registration-form disclosures (DOB, grade, school type) as actual-knowledge triggers. Age volunteered in free-form chat (e.g., 'I'm 10') is not explicitly enumerated in rule text or FAQ — it is a legally reasonable inference but not an explicitly codified trigger.

challenge.correctedStatement: COPPA's actual-knowledge trigger can be activated when a user discloses DOB, grade-level, school type, OR states their age in in-app chat (e.g. "I'm 10") — all four are FTC-confirmed in the official COPPA FAQ, including the chat-announcement scenario (contra the verifier, who wrongly called the chat trigger uncodified). But two caveats: (1) Actual knowledge requires the operator to be AWARE of the disclosure — the FTC FAQ states that if no one in the organization is aware of an age-revealing post, the requisite actual knowledge may not exist. Mere disclosure does not auto-trigger; for an AI tutor that programmatically processes chat, awareness is likely satisfied. (2) Once triggered, VPC is required before collection EXCEPT for the enumerated 16 CFR 312.5(c) exceptions (e.g., collecting parent/child contact info solely to obtain consent, support for internal operations, one-time response).
challenge.reasoning: Attack found errors on BOTH sides. The verifier wrongly downgraded the in-chat trigger as "not explicitly codified" — the FTC FAQ explicitly enumerates the child-announces-age scenario, so all four triggers are confirmed. But both claim and verifier missed two real overstatements: (1) actual knowledge requires organizational AWARENESS of the disclosure, not mere disclosure; (2) VPC-before-collection has codified 312.5(c) exceptions. Core survives (for an AI tutor processing chat, awareness is met), but needs these caveats. Weakened, not overturned.

counterEvidence.source: FTC COPPA FAQ (official) — actual knowledge / child announcing age
counterEvidence.finding: FTC: operator 'may be considered to have actual knowledge where a child announces her age... if you monitor user posts, if a responsible member of your organization sees the post, or if someone alerts you.' Chat trigger IS enumerated — verifier's 'three of four' correction is wrong.

counterEvidence.source: FTC COPPA FAQ (official) — awareness qualifier
counterEvidence.finding: FTC: 'even where the child does reveal age-identifying information, if no one in your organization is aware of the post, then you may not have the requisite actual knowledge.' Trigger needs organizational AWARENESS, not mere disclosure — caveat both claim and verifier missed.

counterEvidence.source: 16 CFR 312.5(c) (eCFR / Cornell LII)
counterEvidence.finding: VPC is required prior to collection EXCEPT enumerated exceptions: parent/child contact info to obtain consent (c)(1), one-time response (c)(3), support for internal operations (c)(7). Claim's 'before any personal-information collection' overstates by omitting codified carve-outs.

counterEvidence.source: Nelson Mullins / FTC Sept 2025 6(b) AI-chatbot inquiry + 2025 COPPA amendments
counterEvidence.finding: FTC Sept 2025 6(b) inquiry assesses chatbot COPPA compliance; 2025 amendments (in force, actual-knowledge standard unchanged) require separate VPC for AI training. Confirms actual-knowledge reach to AI-chat context — supports claim's direction.

counterEvidence.source: FTC 'Not Just for Kids' Sites guidance
counterEvidence.finding: Confirms grade ('What grade are you in?') and school-type questions, plus DOB revealing under-13, are actual-knowledge triggers for general-audience operators. Corroborates three of the four enumerated triggers.

final.statement: COPPA's actual-knowledge trigger can be activated when a user discloses DOB, grade-level, school type, OR states their age in in-app chat (e.g. "I'm 10") — all four are FTC-confirmed in the official COPPA FAQ, including the chat-announcement scenario (contra the verifier, who wrongly called the chat trigger uncodified). But two caveats: (1) Actual knowledge requires the operator to be AWARE of the disclosure — the FTC FAQ states that if no one in the organization is aware of an age-revealing post, the requisite actual knowledge may not exist. Mere disclosure does not auto-trigger; for an AI tutor that programmatically processes chat, awareness is likely satisfied. (2) Once triggered, VPC is required before collection EXCEPT for the enumerated 16 CFR 312.5(c) exceptions (e.g., collecting parent/child contact info solely to obtain consent, support for internal operations, one-time response).
final.reasoning: Attack found errors on BOTH sides. The verifier wrongly downgraded the in-chat trigger as "not explicitly codified" — the FTC FAQ explicitly enumerates the child-announces-age scenario, so all four triggers are confirmed. But both claim and verifier missed two real overstatements: (1) actual knowledge requires organizational AWARENESS of the disclosure, not mere disclosure; (2) VPC-before-collection has codified 312.5(c) exceptions. Core survives (for an AI tutor processing chat, awareness is met), but needs these caveats. Weakened, not overturned.

originalClaim: COPPA's Verifiable Parental Consent unlocks processing for under-13s, with FTC-approved methods enumerated (credit card, government-ID match, knowledge-based authentication, video conference, etc.).

verify.notes: Claim is accurate. The four methods named in the claim (credit card, government-ID match, KBA, video conference) are all genuinely enumerated in 16 CFR 312.5 — the first three were in the 2013 rule, KBA was formally added/clarified in the 2025 amendments. The "etc." correctly implies additional methods exist (print-and-send, toll-free telephone, email-plus, text-plus, facial-recognition-with-photo-ID). The 2025 amendments' full compliance deadline (April 22, 2026) has now passed as of June 5, 2026, so the expanded method list is fully operative. No correction needed.

evidence.source: 16 CFR 312.5 / eCFR (via law.cornell.edu search result)
evidence.finding: 312.5(b)(2) enumerates VPC methods: print-and-send (postal/fax/scan), credit card/debit card with per-transaction notification, toll-free telephone staffed personnel, video-conference with trained personnel, government-ID database check (with prompt deletion), and knowledge-based authentication. All methods cited in the claim are present.

evidence.source: FTC Press Release — 2025 COPPA Final Rule Amendments (Jan 2025)
evidence.finding: 2025 amendments (effective June 23, 2025; full compliance deadline April 22, 2026 — now past as of June 5, 2026) added KBA via dynamic multiple-choice questions, government-issued photo ID with facial recognition (human-reviewed), and text-plus method to the enumerated VPC list. originalClaim: The 2025 COPPA Rule amendments require a written data-retention policy, retention 'no longer than reasonably necessary,' and impose a retention cap on children's data; the actual cap/policy text is unverified (FTC site 403'd).

verify.correction: The three core elements of the claim are confirmed: (1) written retention policy required under 16 CFR 312.10; (2) 'no longer than reasonably necessary' standard (exact text: 'only as long as is reasonably necessary to fulfill the specific purpose(s) for which the information was collected'); (3) prohibition on indefinite retention. However, the characterization of a 'retention cap' is imprecise: 312.10 imposes NO specific numerical cap (no fixed days/months limit). The rule's 'cap' is a purpose-limitation floor plus a prohibition on indefinite retention — operators must set their own specific deletion timeframes in their written policy. The rule is fully in force as of April 22, 2026 (compliance deadline passed).
verify.notes: The claim's hedge ('actual cap/policy text is unverified') was warranted — the FTC site 403'd. Now verified via Cornell LII and secondary sources. The compliance date has now passed (April 22, 2026), so 'in force' status should be updated from uncertain to confirmed. File confidence of 0.7 was appropriately cautious given the 403 gap.

originalClaim: The 2025 COPPA Rule amendments require separate Verifiable Parental Consent for the sale or targeted advertising of children's personal information, even where general VPC already exists.

evidence.source: 16 CFR 312.5(a)(2) — 2025 COPPA Rule Amendments (in force June 23, 2025)

originalClaim: The 2025 COPPA Rule amendments restrict disclosure of children's data to third parties for targeted advertising / behavioural profiling — operating on the data itself, not just the consent.

verify.correction: The 2025 COPPA Rule amendments (16 CFR § 312.5(a)(2), effective June 23 2025) DO restrict third-party disclosure of children's data for targeted advertising/behavioural profiling — but the mechanism is a consent requirement, not a standalone prohibition on the data or conduct itself. Operators must obtain separate verifiable parental consent before making such disclosures; with proper consent, disclosure for targeted advertising remains lawful. An outright data-operation ban (independent of consent) was proposed in the COPPA 2.0 legislation but was never enacted. The only consent-independent restriction added by the 2025 amendments is a retention/deletion limit (§ 312.10), not a targeted-advertising prohibition.
verify.notes: The matrix claim conflates two different regulatory architectures: (a) a consent-gated restriction (what the 2025 rule actually does) vs. (b) a standalone data-operation prohibition (what the claim asserts). The practical effect is broadly similar — targeted advertising without parental opt-in is unlawful — but the legal mechanism differs materially for compliance analysis: there is a lawful pathway via consent. The matrix should update the mechanism description to "requires separate verifiable parental consent (§ 312.5(a)(2)); not an outright ban." Confidence is high; FTC.gov was 403'd but multiple independent law-firm analyses confirm the consent-based structure consistently.

evidence.source: Gibson Dunn — FTC Updates to COPPA Rule (2025 analysis)
evidence.finding: 16 CFR § 312.5(a)(2) requires separate verifiable parental consent before disclosing children's data to third parties for targeted advertising. Both biometric identifiers and government-issued identifiers (SSNs, state ID card numbers, birth certificate numbers, passport numbers) were added to the § 312.2 "personal information" definition. The trusted prior finding (4) confirms the rule is in force. One nuance worth noting: the compliance deadline (April 22, 2026) has now passed as of the 2026-06-05 verification date, meaning the definitional changes are not only effective but fully mandatory for covered operators.

Effective June 23, 2025; compliance deadline April 22, 2026.

originalClaim: FTC §5 (unfair/deceptive practices) binds in the US regardless of COPPA jurisdiction, including for profiling that produces harmful outcomes and for crisis-handling posture if a user is actually a child; there is no child-specific federal profiling statute in the corpus.

verify.notes: All three sub-propositions hold: (1) FTC §5 applies universally to AI-tutoring services (not in any carve-out); (2) FTC has actively extended §5 "unfair practices" framing to harmful outcomes for minors including AI chatbot/crisis contexts; (3) no child-specific federal profiling statute exists — KOSA and COPPA 2.0 remain unenacted bills. Confidence slightly below stated 0.85 is actually higher (0.88) given the primary-source confirmation. No post-2024 development changes this picture.

evidence.source: 15 U.S.C. § 45 (FTC Act §5) — law.cornell.edu
evidence.finding: Section 45(a)(1) prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce universally; its explicit carve-outs cover banks, credit unions, common carriers, air carriers, and Packers & Stockyards entities — not education or AI-tutoring services. No child-specific provision exists in §45 itself.

evidence.source: Perkins Coie — What to Expect from the FTC in Youth Privacy and Online Safety
evidence.finding: FTC positioned to apply §5 'unfair/deceptive' broadly to services involving minors: misrepresented parental controls, deceptive age ratings, AI chatbot investigations for minors, and expansive Section 5 enforcement are all cited enforcement directions for 2025-2026.

evidence.source: Legal Dive / White & Case / Mayer Brown — state AI profiling laws tracker 2025-2026
evidence.finding: Confirmed: in the continued absence of a federal profiling statute, 16 states have enacted AI laws targeting profiling. No comprehensive federal child profiling statute enacted as of mid-2026.

evidence.source: FTC — Final COPPA Rule Amendments, Federal Register (Apr 22, 2025)
evidence.finding: The 2025 COPPA amendments (in force June 23 2025, compliance April 22 2026) remain narrowly scoped to under-13s and to covered operators — distinct from and narrower than FTC §5's general unfair/deceptive-practices authority, confirming the two instruments operate independently.

evidence.source: Congress.gov — KOSA S.1748 and COPPA 2.0, 119th Congress (2025-2026)
evidence.finding: KOSA reintroduced May 2025; passed Senate subcommittee March 2026 but not enacted as of June 2026. COPPA 2.0 (targeted-ad ban for under-17s) also pending. Neither creates a child profiling statute in current federal law.

challenge.correctedStatement: FTC Act §5 (15 U.S.C. §45(a)) binds AI-tutoring services in the US regardless of COPPA jurisdiction, including deceptive crisis-handling/persona conduct toward an actual child and, via the §45(n) unfairness balancing test (substantial injury, not reasonably avoidable, not outweighed by benefits), profiling that produces harmful outcomes — though a pure §5-unfairness theory over harmful-outcome profiling absent deception is a developing, case-by-case application, not settled black-letter law. No standalone comprehensive federal child-profiling statute is enacted (KOSA/KIDS Act H.R. 7757, COPPA 2.0/S.836, CHAT Act all remain bills); however COPPA itself regulates a slice of child profiling by requiring parental consent for persistent identifiers used in behavioral advertising/profiling of under-13s.
challenge.reasoning: All three sub-propositions survive. §5's universal reach (no edtech/AI carve-out) and active FTC chatbot/minor enforcement are confirmed by primary and secondary sources. Two weakening refinements only: (1) §5-unfairness over harmful-outcome profiling needs the §45(n) balancing test and is a developing, not settled, application — 'binds' is slightly strong; (2) COPPA already regulates under-13 profiling via persistent identifiers, so 'no child-specific federal profiling statute' holds only as 'no comprehensive standalone one.' Neither contradicts the claim. Verifier's KOSA path detail was imprecise (KIDS Act, House E&C) but conclusion intact.

counterEvidence.finding: §5 'unfairness' is not a flat ban — it requires substantial injury, not reasonably avoidable, not outweighed by benefits, assessed case-by-case. §5-unfairness over harmful-outcome profiling (absent deception) is a developing/contested application, not settled law. 'Binds' slightly overstates.

counterEvidence.finding: COPPA itself regulates a slice of child profiling: persistent identifiers used for behavioral advertising/profiling of under-13s require verifiable parental consent. So 'no child-specific federal profiling statute' is true only as 'no standalone comprehensive one' — a partial federal child-profiling regime exists.

originalClaim: California's Age-Appropriate Design Code (AB-2273 / CA AADC) has residual obligations for services likely accessed by children, but what remains enforceable after NetChoice v. Bonta is unsettled and needs counsel review.

verify.correction: The claim's core premise (residual obligations exist; what is enforceable is partly unsettled) is directionally correct but materially understates how far the picture has clarified. As of April 3, 2026, the Ninth Circuit mandate is issued and a defined set of AADC provisions ARE actively enforceable against services likely accessed by children: age estimation (§1798.99.31(a)(5)), high-privacy defaults, age-appropriate notices, monitoring/geolocation signals, and privacy tools. What remains enjoined is equally specific: data use restrictions (§§1798.99.31(b)(1)-(4)), dark patterns prohibition (§1798.99.31(b)(7)), and DPIAs — enjoined on First Amendment vagueness grounds, not broad constitutional invalidity. Residual uncertainty exists only on remand issues (age estimation factual record, severability) and potential further NetChoice challenge. The claim should be updated to reflect that a material subset of AADC obligations is now clearly in force, not merely "unsettled," and counsel review should focus on the remanded age-estimation provision and the still-enjoined sections rather than the whole statute.

The March 12, 2026 Ninth Circuit decision (25-2366) resolved the broad injunction, replacing it with a narrower, provision-specific one. The compliance matrix should split AADC into two rows: (A) enforceable obligations (age estimation, defaults, notices, signals — all in force April 3, 2026); (B) enjoined provisions (data use restrictions, dark patterns, DPIAs — blocked pending further proceedings). "Needs counsel review" remains accurate for the remanded issues and any new challenge risk.

Bonta decision (No. 25-2366, mandate April 3, 2026), a defined subset is presently in effect: age estimation (§1798.99.31(a)(5)), high-privacy defaults (a)(6), age-appropriate notices (a)(7), adult-monitoring signal (a)(8), privacy tools (a)(10), geolocation restrictions (b)(5)-(6), and age-estimation data minimization (b)(8). originalClaim: Absent COPPA jurisdiction, US marketing is governed by the CAN-SPAM Act and state consumer-protection laws, which are not child-specific; CAN-SPAM was not researched in this corpus.

verify.correction: The claim has two verifiable components and one framing problem. (1) CONFIRMED: CAN-SPAM contains no child-specific marketing provisions — it is age-neutral commercial-email law. (2) CONFIRMED: CAN-SPAM was not researched in the corpus per the filing's own admission. (3) INCORRECT framing: the claim that 'absent COPPA jurisdiction, US marketing is governed by CAN-SPAM + generic state consumer-protection laws' is materially incomplete. CAN-SPAM covers only commercial email — not advertising, push notifications, in-app promotions, or app-store marketing. More importantly, multiple child-specific US instruments exist beyond COPPA: (a) FTC Act Section 5 UDAP authority directly addresses unfair/deceptive child-targeting practices; (b) the 2025 COPPA Rule amendments (in force June 2025) restrict targeted advertising to under-13s; (c) state child-specific laws now in effect — Maryland Kids Code/MODPA (Oct 2025), NY Child Data Protection Act (June 2025), California AADC (enjoined but active litigation), Florida Social Media Safety Act — are explicitly child-specific, making the claim that state consumer-protection laws are 'not child-specific' factually wrong as of 2026. Corrected statement: 'Absent COPPA jurisdiction, US commercial email is governed by the non-child-specific CAN-SPAM Act; however, broader US marketing to minors is also subject to FTC Act Section 5 UDAP authority, the 2025 COPPA Rule targeted-advertising restrictions, and a growing body of state child-specific laws (Maryland, New York, California, Florida) that directly regulate marketing practices directed at minors.'
verify.notes: The self-admission 'CAN-SPAM not researched in corpus' is accurate and verifiable. The core factual assertion about CAN-SPAM being non-child-specific is correct. The verdict is partially_correct because the claim's framing — treating CAN-SPAM as the primary US marketing governance instrument absent COPPA — both overstates CAN-SPAM's scope (email only, not all marketing) and omits significant child-specific instruments now in force. The matrix should flag: (1) CAN-SPAM scope = commercial email only; (2) FTC Section 5 as a separate child-protective tool; (3) state child-specific laws now create a meaningful child-specific marketing layer at the state level as of 2025-2026.

evidence.source: 15 U.S.C. § 7702 (CAN-SPAM Act definitions), law.cornell.edu
evidence.finding: CAN-SPAM regulates 'commercial electronic mail messages' with no age-specific provisions or child-differentiated rules. Confirmed not child-specific. Its only minor-adjacent provision is labeling requirements for sexually explicit content.

evidence.source: FTC CAN-SPAM Compliance Guide, ftc.gov
evidence.finding: CAN-SPAM governs commercial email only — it does not cover advertising broadly, in-app promotions, push notifications, or non-email marketing channels. Describing it as governing 'US marketing' overstates its scope significantly.

evidence.source: FTC COPPA Rule amendments, Federal Register (2025-04-22)
evidence.finding: The 2025 COPPA Rule (in force June 2025, compliance deadline April 2026) restricts targeted advertising to under-13s — a child-specific federal marketing constraint that applies even where the core COPPA personal-data collection threshold is not met independently.

evidence.source: State children-specific marketing laws survey, NYU Compliance & Enforcement / Mayer Brown 2025
evidence.finding: Multiple state laws (Maryland Kids Code/MODPA eff. Oct 2025, NY CDPA eff. June 2025, California AADC, Florida Social Media Safety Act) are explicitly child-specific marketing/design statutes — the claim that state consumer-protection laws are 'not child-specific' is overbroad.

evidence.source: FTC Act Section 5 UDAP authority over child-targeting practices, FTC.gov
evidence.finding: FTC Section 5 (unfair/deceptive acts) extends to child-targeting marketing practices as an independent child-protective instrument beyond COPPA. The framing 'absent COPPA, only CAN-SPAM + generic state law' omits this authority entirely.

challenge.correctedStatement: Absent COPPA jurisdiction (i.e., not directed to under-13s and no actual knowledge of under-13 data collection), US commercial EMAIL is governed by the age-neutral CAN-SPAM Act — which covers only commercial email, not advertising broadly, push notifications, or in-app/app-store promotion. Marketing to minors more broadly is also subject to FTC Act Section 5 UDAP authority and a growing child-specific STATE layer (NY CDPA, eff. June 2025; Maryland MODPA/Kids Code, eff. Oct 2025; Florida HB3, enforceable pending appeal; California AADC, partly in effect after the 9th Cir. 3/12/2026 ruling) that regulates targeted advertising and design directed at under-18s. NOTE: the 2025 COPPA Rule targeted-advertising restrictions are NOT an independent 'absent-COPPA' instrument — they are COPPA and apply only within COPPA jurisdiction. CAN-SPAM itself remains correctly characterized as not child-specific.
challenge.reasoning: Original claim's CAN-SPAM facts hold but its framing overstates scope and ignores a now-real child-specific state layer — so it stays weakened. The verifier's core corrections are sound, but it over-reached: citing the 2025 COPPA amendment as a child-specific tool that applies 'absent COPPA jurisdiction' is internally contradictory — COPPA amendments only bite within COPPA jurisdiction. The state laws are privacy/design statutes, not classic UDAP 'consumer-protection' law, and two are litigation-contingent. Corrected statement fixes both the claim's and the verifier's errors.

counterEvidence.source: FTC COPPA FAQ / 16 CFR 312.3 jurisdictional trigger (per multiple 2025 amendment analyses)
counterEvidence.finding: VERIFIER ERROR: the 2025 COPPA targeted-ad restriction applies ONLY to operators 'directed to children' under 13 or with 'actual knowledge' of under-13 collection. It is COPPA — it cannot govern conduct the claim defines as 'absent COPPA jurisdiction.'

counterEvidence.source: FTC CAN-SPAM Compliance Guide + 15 U.S.C. 7702
counterEvidence.finding: CONFIRMED: CAN-SPAM covers commercial email only, age-neutral; push notifications and in-app marketing fall outside its scope. Treating it as governing 'US marketing' overstates scope. Verifier correct here.

counterEvidence.source: NY Child Data Protection Act (eff. June 20, 2025); Maryland MODPA/Kids Code (eff. Oct 1, 2025)
counterEvidence.finding: CONFIRMED child-specific state marketing layer: NY CDPA bars targeted ads to under-18s absent consent; MODPA bars selling/targeted-ads on under-18 data ('knew or should have known'). These are privacy/design statutes, not classic UDAP 'consumer-protection' law.

counterEvidence.finding: CA AADC injunction NARROWED 3/12/2026 (age-estimation revived; data-use/dark-pattern bars still enjoined) — partly active, not fully enjoined. FL HB3 enforceable pending appeal. Both child-specific; both litigation-contingent, so verifier's flat 'in force' framing needs caveat.

counterEvidence.finding: CONFIRMED: Section 5 unfair/deceptive authority independently reaches child-targeting marketing regardless of COPPA jurisdiction; the original claim omits it. Verifier correct.

Oct 2025; Florida HB3, enforceable pending appeal; California AADC, partly in effect after the 9th Cir. 3/12/2026 ruling) that regulates targeted advertising and design directed at under-18s. NOTE: the 2025 COPPA Rule targeted-advertising restrictions are NOT an independent 'absent-COPPA' instrument — they are COPPA and apply only within COPPA jurisdiction. CAN-SPAM itself remains correctly characterized as not child-specific.

The state laws are privacy/design statutes, not classic UDAP 'consumer-protection' law, and two are litigation-contingent. Corrected statement fixes both the claim's and the verifier's errors.

originalClaim: The FTC issued a 6(b) inquiry in September 2025 targeting AI-chatbot operators, signalling that crisis handling should be structural rather than improvised; no specific standard on age-vulnerability or emotion-inference exists yet, and the 6(b) order text is unverified (FTC 403'd).

verify.notes: The claim is accurate on all verifiable dimensions: September 2025 date, 6(b) mechanism (information-gathering, not rulemaking), no specific age-vulnerability or emotion-inference standard, and FTC 403 blocking direct order-text verification. One scope nuance worth flagging: the inquiry targets AI companion chatbots (emotional-connection products) specifically — not AI tutoring apps — so the signal for an ed-tech context is indirect. The "structural rather than improvised" characterization is an interpretive gloss on the inquiry's topics, not a direct FTC quote, but it is fair given that the order explicitly asks about crisis protocols and testing for psychological harms to minors.

evidence.source: FTC press release (via search results; direct ftc.gov fetch returns 403)
evidence.finding: FTC issued Section 6(b) orders to seven AI companion chatbot operators on September 11, 2025, requesting information on safety, testing, age restrictions, and child/teen protection measures. Confirmed real and correctly dated.

evidence.source: DLA Piper secondary summary of 6(b) order (fetched 2026-06-05)
evidence.finding: The 6(b) study is explicitly not a standard-setting action — it requests detailed information to inform later enforcement or rulemaking. No specific standards on age-vulnerability or emotion-inference are established. Purely information-gathering.

evidence.source: FKKS Advertising Law Blog summary (fetched 2026-06-05)
evidence.finding: Order covers pre/post-deployment testing for emotional/psychological harms especially to minors, crisis-adjacent safety measures, and parental disclosures — consistent with signalling structural crisis-handling expectations, without mandating a specific standard.

evidence.source: FTC.gov primary URL (attempted direct fetch)
evidence.finding: FTC.gov returns HTTP 403 Forbidden on both the press-release and the 6(b) order report page, corroborating the matrix's own note that order text is unverified due to FTC 403.

evidence.source: Hunton Andrews Kurth / Nelson Mullins summaries (fetched 2026-06-05)
evidence.finding: Multiple law firm alerts consistently describe the inquiry as fact-finding only — no regulatory standard imposed. Inquiry targets companion chatbots specifically (emotional connection/companionship products), not AI tutoring apps, though the regulatory signal is broader.

challenge.correctedStatement: The FTC issued Section 6(b) information-gathering orders on Sept 11, 2025 to seven AI COMPANION-chatbot operators (not tutoring apps), signalling that crisis handling should be structural; the 6(b) itself sets no standard, and direct order-text verification is blocked (FTC 403'd). However, "no specific standard on age-vulnerability or emotion-inference exists yet" is overstated as of 2026-06-05: binding crisis-protocol/minor-protection standards are now in force — California SB 243 (Jan 1, 2026) and New York GBL §1700 (Nov 5, 2025) for "companion chatbots," whose definitions can reach an emotionally-supportive ed-tech tutor since educational services are NOT excluded — and the EU AI Act Art 5(1)(f) emotion-inference prohibition in education has been in force since Feb 2, 2025 (B2C home-use tutor likely outside its institutional scope, but the standard exists).
challenge.reasoning: Verifiable spine holds: Sept-2025 date, 6(b) mechanism, companion-chatbot scope, FTC 403 (re-confirmed live). But the verifier's 0.88 "confirmed" missed three live standards that defeat "no specific standard...exists yet": CA SB 243 (in force Jan 1 2026), NY GBL §1700 (Nov 5 2025), and EU AI Act Art 5(1)(f) emotion-inference-in-education (Feb 2 2025). SB 243's exclusion omits education, so an emotionally-supportive tutor may be covered — the ed-tech signal is no longer merely "indirect." Material weakening, not fatal: FTC spine + 403 accurate; state-law coverage of a pure tutor is uncertain, not certain.

counterEvidence.source: California SB 243 (Perkins Coie; FPF; Crowell & Moring) — in force
counterEvidence.finding: Effective Jan 1, 2026: mandatory crisis/suicide protocols, AI-disclosure + 3-hour break reminders for known minors. originalClaim: In the US, the '3b posture' (rebuttable presumption of parental consent from account existence such as Family Sharing/Family Link) is 'unclear, not no'; the best-available argument is engineered ignorance (no DOB/grade/age-capture) plus reliance on COPPA's actual-knowledge ignorance defense — a posture, not a safe harbor, with named cases (Wildec, TikTok) cited but unverified.

verify.correction: The claim's core legal framing is substantially correct: (1) Family Sharing/Family Link as a rebuttable presumption of VPC is genuinely 'unclear, not no' under COPPA — the FTC explicitly declined a rebuttable-presumption mechanism in rulemaking; (2) the no-DOB/no-age-capture posture maps to the actual-knowledge ignorance defense; (3) it is correctly characterized as a posture, not a safe harbor. Two corrections are needed: (a) The February 2026 FTC enforcement discretion statement on age verification (post-matrix) actively encourages operators to conduct age verification, which meaningfully shifts the risk posture of deliberate non-collection — the matrix should note this development. (b) Wildec (2019 FTC warning letter) and TikTok (active DOJ/FTC litigation, no final judgment as of mid-2026) are valid illustrative citations for the actual-knowledge standard, but both show the FTC finding actual knowledge FROM age signals the operators DID collect — they do not directly support the 'no-collection' variant of the engineered-ignorance argument. The matrix should clarify that these cases demonstrate the enforcement floor (knowledge from signals you collect), not validation of the no-collection escape route.
verify.notes: The '3b posture' framing ('unclear, not no') is legally sound and well-calibrated. The main gap is the Feb 2026 FTC age-verification policy statement, which post-dates the matrix and creates pressure on the no-collection approach. The FTC's willful-disregard doctrine is also a live risk the matrix should surface: if the app's analytics or marketing reveal a child-heavy audience, no-collection is insufficient to defeat an 'actual knowledge' finding. Wildec and TikTok citations are directionally valid but prove a slightly different point than stated. Confidence 0.75 in the original matrix is appropriate given these nuances.

evidence.source: FTC COPPA FAQ + 16 CFR 312 (eCFR) + 2025 Rule amendments (April 22, 2026 compliance deadline)
evidence.finding: COPPA's actual-knowledge standard is confirmed: general-audience operators have no obligation to ask age. Age-neutral screening allows reliance on self-reported age. The FTC explicitly declined to permit a rebuttable presumption based on family device/account sharing, finding it 'difficult to reliably distinguish' adult from child users. The 'unclear, not no' characterization for Family Sharing/Family Link as VPC is accurate.

evidence.source: FTC COPPA Enforcement Policy Statement (Feb 25, 2026) — age-verification enforcement discretion
evidence.finding: Post-matrix development: FTC will not enforce COPPA Rule against operators who collect personal data solely to verify age, if data is deleted promptly. This pressures the 'engineered ignorance' (no-collection) posture — FTC is now actively encouraging age verification rather than treating no-collection as neutral. Does not eliminate the no-collection defense but meaningfully shifts the enforcement climate.

evidence.source: FTC press release + DOJ complaint, US v. TikTok/ByteDance (Aug 2, 2024); motion to dismiss denied Nov 2025
evidence.finding: TikTok's 'actual knowledge' was shown via internal communications confirming underage users and inaction. The case is active litigation (not a settled precedent) as of mid-2026. Valid illustration of the actual-knowledge standard, but citing it as case-law support overstates its weight — it is an ongoing enforcement complaint, not a judicial ruling.

evidence.source: FTC warning letter to Wildec LLC (May 2019)
evidence.finding: Wildec was an FTC warning letter, not a consent decree or settlement. The FTC found 'actual knowledge' because the app solicited birthdates at registration (opposite of no-collection). Wildec illustrates actual-knowledge enforcement, not the 'engineered ignorance' defense. Citing it in that context is somewhat misleading.

evidence.source: FTC willful-disregard doctrine (cited in COPPA rulemaking record and Georgetown Law Tech Institute paper, Sep 2025)
evidence.finding: The FTC treats 'willful disregard' as a subset of actual knowledge (operators who 'blind themselves' to age evidence). This means the no-collection posture is not a safe harbor: if analytics, content, or marketing patterns signal a child audience, the FTC can still find constructive/willful-disregard knowledge even absent DOB collection.

challenge.correctedStatement: US 3b posture is "unclear, not no": FTC's 2025 rule declined a broad rebuttable VPC presumption from device/account sharing (limited rebuttal only via the mixed-audience path), so Family Sharing/Family Link is not a sanctioned VPC method but not categorically barred. No-DOB/no-age-capture maps to COPPA's actual-knowledge trigger — general-audience operators need not ask age; FTC expressly rejected a "reason to know" standard — but actual knowledge folds in willful disregard, so it is a posture, not a safe harbor. Wildec (2019 warning letter) and TikTok (active DOJ/FTC suit, MTD largely denied Nov 6 2025, no final judgment) are real but show actual knowledge from signals operators DID collect — they prove the enforcement floor, not the no-collection escape. Two caveats: (a) the Feb 25 2026 FTC policy statement is permissive enforcement discretion for operators who voluntarily age-verify without VPC — it imposes NO new duty on non-collectors and does not change actual knowledge; it signals FTC's preference for verification but does NOT legally erode the no-collection posture; (b) because minors are among the users, the dispositive risk pivot is the directed-to-children / mixed-audience classification — if content/marketing reads as child-directed, the engineered-ignorance posture is materially weaker.
challenge.reasoning: Primary sources confirm every load-bearing element: declined rebuttable presumption, actual-knowledge trigger, rejected reason-to-know, willful-disregard floor, and the real but illustrative Wildec/TikTok cases. The claim and the verifier's correction (b) survive. The verifier's correction (a) overstates: the Feb 2026 statement is a carrot for voluntary verifiers, not a stick against non-collectors, and changes no legal standard. Underweighted by both: the directed-to-children/mixed-audience classification is the real risk pivot for a minor-using tutor app.

counterEvidence.source: FTC COPPA Age-Verification Policy Statement (Feb 25, 2026) + Mayer Brown/Cooley analyses
counterEvidence.finding: Pure enforcement discretion for operators who voluntarily verify age without VPC; imposes no obligation on non-collectors and does not alter the actual-knowledge standard. Verifier's 'meaningfully shifts non-collection risk' overstates its legal effect — it is a carrot, not a stick.

counterEvidence.source: FTC COPPA FAQ + 2024/2025 rulemaking record (Federal Register, eCFR 16 CFR 312)
counterEvidence.finding: General-audience operators need not ask age; actual knowledge includes willful disregard; FTC expressly REJECTED a 'reason to know' constructive standard. Confirms posture-not-safe-harbor AND that the verifier's 'audience-pattern willful disregard' caveat must not drift into the rejected reason-to-know test.

counterEvidence.source: FTC 2025 COPPA Final Rule (eff. June 23 2025; compliance Apr 22 2026); State AG comments
counterEvidence.finding: FTC declined a broad rebuttable VPC presumption from device/account sharing; State AGs opposed it absent measures beyond logged-in/reauth status. Limited rebuttal survives only via the mixed-audience category. Confirms 'unclear, not no.'

counterEvidence.source: US v. TikTok/ByteDance (C.D. Cal.); MTD largely denied Nov 6, 2025 (Judge Wu)
counterEvidence.finding: Active litigation, no final judgment as of mid-2026; actual knowledge alleged via internal comms re: known underage users. Confirms verifier: valid as illustration of the actual-knowledge floor, not a judicial precedent and not validation of the no-collection variant.

counterEvidence.source: FTC warning letter to Wildec LLC (May 6, 2019)
counterEvidence.finding: Warning letter (not consent decree). Apps solicited DOB and exposed self-identified under-13 users — actual knowledge from collected signals, the opposite of no-collection. Confirms verifier correction (b): proves enforcement floor, not the engineered-ignorance escape.

final.statement: US 3b posture is "unclear, not no": FTC's 2025 rule declined a broad rebuttable VPC presumption from device/account sharing (limited rebuttal only via the mixed-audience path), so Family Sharing/Family Link is not a sanctioned VPC method but not categorically barred. No-DOB/no-age-capture maps to COPPA's actual-knowledge trigger — general-audience operators need not ask age; FTC expressly rejected a "reason to know" standard — but actual knowledge folds in willful disregard, so it is a posture, not a safe harbor. Wildec (2019 warning letter) and TikTok (active DOJ/FTC suit, MTD largely denied Nov 6 2025, no final judgment) are real but show actual knowledge from signals operators DID collect — they prove the enforcement floor, not the no-collection escape. Two caveats: (a) the Feb 25 2026 FTC policy statement is permissive enforcement discretion for operators who voluntarily age-verify without VPC — it imposes NO new duty on non-collectors and does not change actual knowledge; it signals FTC's preference for verification but does NOT legally erode the no-collection posture; (b) because minors are among the users, the dispositive risk pivot is the directed-to-children / mixed-audience classification — if content/marketing reads as child-directed, the engineered-ignorance posture is materially weaker.
final.reasoning: Primary sources confirm every load-bearing element: declined rebuttable presumption, actual-knowledge trigger, rejected reason-to-know, willful-disregard floor, and the real but illustrative Wildec/TikTok cases. The claim and the verifier's correction (b) survive. The verifier's correction (a) overstates: the Feb 2026 statement is a carrot for voluntary verifiers, not a stick against non-collectors, and changes no legal standard. Underweighted by both: the directed-to-children/mixed-audience classification is the real risk pivot for a minor-using tutor app.

originalClaim: The UK Children's Code Standard on profiling requires profiling to be off-by-default for under-18s; consent cannot unlock it by default.

verify.correction: The substantive rule is correct: UK Children's Code Standard 12 (Profiling) requires profiling to be off by default for under-18s, and consent alone cannot unlock it — a compelling reason demonstrating the best interests of the child is also required, making default-on impermissible even with consent. However, the claim (and file matrix) mis-numbers the standard as 13; profiling is Standard 12. Standard 13 is 'Nudge techniques.' The claim as stated is substantively accurate but cites the wrong standard number.
verify.notes: ICO's own website 403'd for all direct page fetches; standard number confirmed via ICO URL path structure (snippet), two independent compliance-guide secondaries, and the FAQ page snippet. The 'consent cannot unlock it' framing in the claim is slightly imprecise: consent is necessary but not sufficient — even post-consent, profiling requires a best-interests compelling-reason analysis and harm-prevention measures. The claim's 'by default' qualifier (consent cannot unlock it BY DEFAULT) is accurate. Matrix should correct the standard number from 13 to 12 and note Standard 13 = Nudge techniques.

evidence.source: ICO Children's Code — Standard 12 (Profiling), via evalian.co.uk/childrens-code/ and staylegal.co.uk/the-15-standards-of-the-childrens-code/
evidence.finding: Profiling standard in the UK Children's Code is Standard 12 (not 13). It requires profiling to be switched off by default unless a compelling reason can be shown, taking account of the best interests of the child. Came into force September 2021.

evidence.source: ICO Children's Code Standard 12, multiple secondary corroborations (ondato.com, evalian.co.uk)
evidence.finding: Consent alone cannot unlock profiling by default. The platform must demonstrate a 'compelling reason that profiling is in the child's best interest.' This is a higher bar than consent; profiling cannot be on-by-default even if the child consents.

evidence.finding: Full list confirmed: 1=best interests, 2=DPIA, 3=age-appropriate application, 4=transparency, 5=detrimental use, 6=policies, 7=default settings, 8=data minimisation, 9=data sharing, 10=geolocation, 11=parental controls, 12=profiling, 13=nudge techniques, 14=connected toys, 15=online tools.

originalClaim: UK Children's Code standards include data minimisation, no detrimental use of children's data, no nudge techniques, and a 'best interests of the child' duty that binds the moment an operator has any signal a user is a child; consent (parental or self) does not unlock these standards.

verify.correction: The four named standards (data minimisation, detrimental use, nudge techniques, best interests) are confirmed. Consent (parental or self) correctly does not unlock or override these standards. However, the trigger description is imprecise: the Code does not bind an operator "the moment" they have "any signal" a user is a child. The Code applies at service level when a service is "likely to be accessed by children" (more probable than not that a significant number of children access it). For individual users, Standard 3 requires operators to either establish age with appropriate certainty or apply the standards to all users — meaning the practical effect can be broad, but the operative trigger is a service-level likelihood assessment, not a per-user reactive signal. The claim's description of trigger mechanics slightly overstates immediacy and per-user reactivity.
verify.notes: ICO.org.uk returned HTTP 403 for all direct page fetches; claim confirmed via search-extracted text, third-party legal commentary (DWT, Harper James, Taylor Wessing), and the recent Reddit £14.47m enforcement (Feb 2026). The consent-does-not-unlock point is well-established. The matrix should update the trigger description from "any signal" framing to "service-level likelihood + apply-to-all fallback" to avoid over-claiming per-user reactive binding.

evidence.source: ICO Children's Code — 15 Standards (search-confirmed, direct page 403'd)

evidence.finding: The Code applies at service level when children form a 'substantive and identifiable user group' (more probable than not). Individual per-user 'any signal' triggering is not the standard; the fallback is to apply standards to all users when age cannot be determined.

challenge.correctedStatement: The UK Children's Code (statutory under DPA 2018 s.123) imposes mandatory design standards — including data minimisation (Std 8), no detrimental use of children's data (Std 5), no nudge techniques that erode privacy (Std 13), and a 'best interests of the child' primary-consideration duty (Std 1). These standards are not unlocked or overridden by consent (parental or self). The Code's operative trigger is service-level: it binds a service 'likely to be accessed by children' (more than a de minimis number of child users). Under Standard 3 an operator must either establish age with risk-appropriate certainty or apply the standards to all users — so for an AI-tutoring app with minors among its users, in-scope status is near-certain. The claim's framing that the duty binds 'the moment an operator has any signal a user is a child' overstates per-user reactive immediacy; the correct mechanic is a service-level likelihood assessment plus an apply-to-all fallback.
