originalClaim: DSA Art 28(2) bans profiling-based advertising to minors and applies to Norway via EEA incorporation (timeline counsel-to-confirm); it binds regardless of consent.

verify.correction: DSA Art 28(2) correctly prohibits profiling-based advertising to minors with no consent exception. However, as of 2026-06-05, the DSA has NOT yet been formally incorporated into the EEA Agreement — no EEA Joint Committee decision has been adopted. Norway is in national consultation phase (July–Oct 2025) targeting summer 2026 application, pending a formal Joint Committee decision. The claim's framing that it 'applies to Norway via EEA incorporation' overstates the current position; the parenthetical '(timeline counsel-to-confirm)' should read as 'NOT YET incorporated; target summer 2026, Joint Committee decision pending.'
verify.notes: The substantive legal rule in Art 28(2) is confirmed accurate: absolute ban on profiling-based ads to known minors, no consent exception. The sole material error is the EEA-Norway incorporation status — it has not occurred yet as of the verification date. Matrix confidence of 0.65 was appropriately cautious. Recommend raising the 'unverified' flag in the EEA cell to 'NOT YET IN FORCE — Norway target summer 2026.' No post-Aug-2025 primary source found confirming a Joint Committee decision.

evidence.source: DSA Art 28(2), Regulation (EU) 2022/2065, dsa-library.com verified text

originalClaim: Norway's Datatilsynet's general posture is that age assurance must be effective, not nominal, with the controller bearing the verification burden; no platform-based age-signal guidance has been located, and the personopplysningsloven §5 actual-knowledge trigger is broader than COPPA.

verify.correction: Three sub-claims are accurate: (1) Datatilsynet's general posture does treat age assurance as a controller burden requiring effective rather than nominal measures (supported by 2025 consultation response and 2026 mapping report); (2) no platform-based age-signal guidance has been located. The third sub-claim is wrong: personopplysningsloven §5 contains NO actual-knowledge trigger of any kind — it is a single sentence setting the consent age at 13. The correct comparison is GDPR Article 8(2), incorporated directly into Norwegian law, which imposes a proactive 'reasonable measures' verification burden on controllers — which is stricter than COPPA's reactive actual-knowledge standard. The claim misattributes the comparison to §5 and mischaracterises the mechanism as an 'actual-knowledge trigger'; it should read: 'GDPR Art. 8(2) as applied in Norway imposes a proactive verification burden stricter than COPPA's actual-knowledge safe harbour.'
verify.notes: The matrix should correct the §5 citation and framing. §5 only sets the age threshold (13); all verification/burden language derives from GDPR Art. 8(2). The 'broader than COPPA' direction is correct (GDPR Art. 8 is stricter) but the mechanism is opposite: GDPR is proactive-burden; COPPA is reactive-knowledge-trigger. There is no Datatilsynet document that explicitly uses the phrase 'effective, not nominal' — that is a fair synthesis of their 2025–2026 posture but should not be cited as a named document.

evidence.source: Datatilsynet – høringssvar om forslag til lov om aldersgrenser i sosiale medier (2025)
evidence.finding: Datatilsynet states platforms must bear the age-verification burden and warns that allowing platforms to self-select methods risks inadequate protection — directionally confirms 'effective not nominal' posture and controller-side burden, but uses no such explicit phrasing.

evidence.source: Datatilsynet – internasjonal kartlegging av barns personvern i nettjenester (2026)
evidence.finding: Datatilsynet's 2026 international mapping found 72% of evaluated sites had bypassable age-verification (self-declaration). Characterized as 'bekymringsverdige' (concerning), reinforcing the posture that nominal self-declaration is insufficient.

evidence.source: Lovdata – personopplysningsloven §5 (English translation NLE/lov/2018-06-15-38)
evidence.finding: §5 contains only a single sentence setting digital consent age at 13 under GDPR Art. 8(1). It contains no 'actual knowledge' trigger, no verification burden provision, and no language comparable to COPPA's knowledge standard.

evidence.source: Lovdata – GDPR Article 8 as incorporated into Norwegian law (NL/lov/2018-06-15-38/gdpr/ARTIKKEL_8)
evidence.finding: Art. 8(2) requires controllers to take 'reasonable measures' to verify parental consent given available technology — a proactive burden. This IS broader/stricter than COPPA's reactive actual-knowledge standard, but the operative provision is Art. 8, not §5.

evidence.source: Datatilsynet – aldersverifisering i sosiale medier (2026 news item)
evidence.finding: No platform-based age-signal guidance has been published by Datatilsynet. The 2026 articles and consultation responses contain no guidance on using platform-provided age signals as an assurance mechanism — confirming that sub-claim.

challenge.correctedStatement: The authoritative EU posture (EDPB Statement 1/2025 on Age Assurance, adopted 11 Feb 2025, which Datatilsynet follows) is that age assurance must be reliable/robust/bypass-resistant (effective, not nominal) AND least-intrusive/proportionate (risk-based, DPIA-gated). Datatilsynet's own 2025-2026 documents support this direction (72% of mapped services had bypassable self-declaration = "concerning") but also caution that aggressive age verification intrudes on all users' privacy — so "controller bears an effective-verification burden" is a fair synthesis, not a stated Datatilsynet rule. No platform-based age-signal guidance has been located (confirmed). The §5 sub-claim is wrong: personopplysningsloven §5 only sets the digital-consent age at 13; it contains no actual-knowledge trigger. The proactive verification burden lives in GDPR Art 8(2) ("rimelige tiltak"/reasonable measures to verify parental consent, qualified by available technology), incorporated into Norwegian law — and that burden runs specifically to verifying parental consent for sub-age users. GDPR Art 8(2) is stricter than COPPA's reactive actual-knowledge standard FOR GENERAL-AUDIENCE services; for child-directed/mixed-audience services (which an AI tutor may be) COPPA imposes affirmative duties regardless of knowledge, so the "broader than COPPA" framing is actor-dependent.
challenge.reasoning: Verifier's §5 correction is correct and survives: §5 sets age 13; the proactive burden is GDPR Art 8(2). Sub-claim 2 (no platform-signal guidance) confirmed. But two material gaps remain: (a) direct reads show Datatilsynet's stance is more cautious than "controller bears effective-verification burden" — the real authority is EDPB Statement 1/2025, cited by neither; (b) the "broader than COPPA" framing is actor-dependent — COPPA's actual-knowledge applies to general-audience only, while child-directed/mixed-audience services owe duties regardless. Claim survives with these caveats; weakened, not overturned.

counterEvidence.source: EDPB Statement 1/2025 on Age Assurance (adopted 11 Feb 2025)
counterEvidence.finding: The authoritative source for 'effective not nominal': age assurance must be reliable, robust, bypass-resistant — but ALSO least-intrusive, proportionate, risk-based and DPIA-gated. Neither claim nor verifier cited it; it is more on-point than Datatilsynet's own docs.

counterEvidence.source: Datatilsynet høringssvar – aldersgrenser i sosiale medier (2025)
counterEvidence.finding: Direct read shows Datatilsynet does NOT assign verification burden to controllers; it cautions age verification 'er et inngrep i personvernet til alle' and urges further study before mandates. The 'controller bears the burden' posture is weaker/softer than the claim asserts.

counterEvidence.source: Lovdata – GDPR Art 8(2) as incorporated (NL/2018-06-15-38/gdpr/ARTIKKEL_8)
counterEvidence.finding: Verbatim: controller must 'treffe rimelige tiltak for å kontrollere at samtykke er gitt eller godkjent av den som har foreldreansvar.' Confirms the burden lives in Art 8(2), runs specifically to verifying PARENTAL CONSENT, and is qualified by available technology — not a freestanding 'verify everyone' mandate. §5 only sets age 13.

counterEvidence.source: FTC FAQ + Feb 25 2026 COPPA Policy Statement (Hunton)
counterEvidence.finding: COPPA's actual-knowledge standard applies only to general-audience services; child-directed/mixed-audience services owe affirmative duties regardless of knowledge. The Feb 2026 policy ENCOURAGES (not requires) age verification and does not change the actual-knowledge standard, so 'GDPR broader' holds only for general-audience actors.

final.statement: The authoritative EU posture (EDPB Statement 1/2025 on Age Assurance, adopted 11 Feb 2025, which Datatilsynet follows) is that age assurance must be reliable/robust/bypass-resistant (effective, not nominal) AND least-intrusive/proportionate (risk-based, DPIA-gated). Datatilsynet's own 2025-2026 documents support this direction (72% of mapped services had bypassable self-declaration = "concerning") but also caution that aggressive age verification intrudes on all users' privacy — so "controller bears an effective-verification burden" is a fair synthesis, not a stated Datatilsynet rule. No platform-based age-signal guidance has been located (confirmed). The §5 sub-claim is wrong: personopplysningsloven §5 only sets the digital-consent age at 13; it contains no actual-knowledge trigger. The proactive verification burden lives in GDPR Art 8(2) ("rimelige tiltak"/reasonable measures to verify parental consent, qualified by available technology), incorporated into Norwegian law — and that burden runs specifically to verifying parental consent for sub-age users. GDPR Art 8(2) is stricter than COPPA's reactive actual-knowledge standard FOR GENERAL-AUDIENCE services; for child-directed/mixed-audience services (which an AI tutor may be) COPPA imposes affirmative duties regardless of knowledge, so the "broader than COPPA" framing is actor-dependent.
final.reasoning: Verifier's §5 correction is correct and survives: §5 sets age 13; the proactive burden is GDPR Art 8(2). Sub-claim 2 (no platform-signal guidance) confirmed. But two material gaps remain: (a) direct reads show Datatilsynet's stance is more cautious than "controller bears effective-verification burden" — the real authority is EDPB Statement 1/2025, cited by neither; (b) the "broader than COPPA" framing is actor-dependent — COPPA's actual-knowledge applies to general-audience only, while child-directed/mixed-audience services owe duties regardless. Claim survives with these caveats; weakened, not overturned.

originalClaim: Datatilsynet issued a June 2024 Nordic DPA declaration addressing 'children's data protection in gaming, AI'; its text was not retrieved and is flagged unverified.

verify.correction: The Oslo Declaration on children's data protection in gaming, AI, and administrative fines was issued on 30–31 May 2024 (not June 2024). Its full text is publicly available on datatilsynet.dk and datatilsynet.no — it was not unverified at the time the matrix was authored; it simply was not fetched. The declaration is a joint Nordic DPA instrument (all seven Nordic DPAs), not a unilateral Datatilsynet document. The topic description in the claim ('children's data protection in gaming, AI') is accurate.
verify.notes: The matrix flagged this as 'unverified' and the text 'not retrieved,' but the document is fully publicly accessible at no fewer than two official DPA domains. The only factual error in the claim is the month: May not June 2024. The claim's 0.55 confidence was overly conservative; the declaration is well-attested. No post-issuance supersession found — the Oslo Declaration remains the current Nordic DPA position on this topic as of 2026-06-05.

evidence.source: Datatilsynet.no — official news article (primary)
evidence.finding: The declaration was issued at the Nordic DPA annual meeting held 30–31 May 2024 in Oslo (not June 2024). It is named the 'Oslo Declaration.' All Nordic DPAs (Norway, Denmark, Finland, Iceland, Sweden, Faroe Islands, Åland) signed. Topics: children's data in gaming, AI/GDPR, and administrative fines.

evidence.source: Oslo Declaration PDF (datatilsynet.dk hosted non-signed version)
evidence.finding: Full text of the Oslo Declaration is publicly accessible. PDF binary; text unextractable by tool, but its existence and hosting by Danish Datatilsynet confirms the document was issued and published.

evidence.finding: Companion document to the Oslo Declaration: joint Nordic DPA principles on children and online gaming, published alongside the May 2024 declaration. Confirms the 'gaming' component of the claim topic.

evidence.source: Digital Policy Alert — event tracker
evidence.finding: Independent tracker records the declaration date as 31 May 2024, corroborating the Datatilsynet primary source and confirming the 'June 2024' date in the claim is incorrect by one month.

challenge.correctedStatement: The 'Oslo Declaration' on children's data protection in gaming, AI, and administrative fines is a joint, non-binding cooperation declaration of all seven Nordic DPAs (Denmark, Faroe Islands, Finland, Iceland, Norway, Sweden, Aland), adopted at their Oslo annual meeting on 30-31 May 2024 and published in early June 2024 (Datatilsynet news 3 June 2024; companion 'Principles on Children & Online Gaming' titled June 2024). Its full text is publicly available on datatilsynet.no and datatilsynet.dk — so the matrix's 'unverified/text not retrieved' flag is unwarranted; the document simply was not fetched. The claim's 'June 2024' is defensible (it matches the publication and companion-document dating). A successor 'Stockholm Declaration' (21-22 May 2025) exists, but its scope shifted to AI/law-enforcement and did not withdraw the 2024 children/gaming principles, which remain the standing Nordic position. The instrument is high-level inter-DPA cooperation, not enforceable guidance directly binding on a consumer AI-tutoring app.
challenge.reasoning: Verifier's core is right: the document exists, is the Oslo Declaration, is publicly accessible, and the topic matches — so the matrix's 'unverified' flag is properly overturned. But two verifier over-assertions need caveats. 'May not June 2024' is overstated: the news page is dated 3 June 2024 and the companion principles PDF is titled 'June 2024,' so the claim's month is defensible, not erroneous. And 'no supersession found' is wrong — a 2025 Stockholm Declaration exists (different scope; 2024 children's principles not withdrawn). Also flag: it's non-binding inter-DPA cooperation, not enforceable app guidance. Claim-plus-correction survives.

originalClaim: Norway's '3b posture' (rebuttable presumption from account existence) is 'unclear, not no' — mirroring the US 3b posture; Datatilsynet has neither blessed nor rejected platform-side consent signals (absence-of-guidance), and engineered ignorance is an arguable posture, not a safe harbor.

verify.correction: The core characterization — Datatilsynet has neither blessed nor rejected platform-side consent signals, and 'engineered ignorance' is arguable but not a safe harbor — is accurate as a description of regulatory silence. However, two details require correction: (1) The US 'COPPA 3b posture' is not a clean rebuttable-presumption-from-account-existence doctrine — the FTC explicitly declined to create such a presumption in 2024, so the 'mirroring' framing overstates the parallel. Norway's situation is more accurately pure silence vs. the US's developed actual-knowledge doctrine. (2) EDPB Statement 1/2025 (Feb 2025, post-matrix) explicitly disfavors self-declaration and pushes risk-based affirmative age assurance, which moves the needle slightly against 'engineered ignorance as arguable' — the space is narrower than the 0.65 confidence implies.
verify.notes: The 'rebuttable presumption from account existence' framing is not found in any primary source for Norway or the US. It appears to be a compliance-framework construct used in the matrix's synthesis layer, not a doctrine any regulator has articulated. Datatilsynet silence is real and confirmed. The Norway-mirrors-US parallel is conceptually weak: GDPR Art. 8 + EDPB 2025 statement together provide more structural pressure against avoidance designs than COPPA's actual-knowledge standard does. Matrix confidence of 0.65 is reasonable but the Norway-US parallel should be flagged as an imprecise analogy.

evidence.source: Norwegian Personal Data Act §5 (Lovdata NLE/lov/2018-06-15-38)
evidence.finding: §5 sets digital consent age at 13 (minimum GDPR allows). The Act contains no 'rebuttable presumption' from account existence, no provision on platform-side consent signals, and no guidance on what controllers must do when they cannot verify user age. The gap is real but the statutory text offers no anchor for the posture.

evidence.source: GDPR Article 8 (gdpr-info.eu)
evidence.finding: Art. 8(2) requires 'reasonable efforts' to verify parental consent. No rebuttable-presumption language exists. Deliberately structuring a service to avoid determining user age would likely fail the reasonable-efforts standard — 'engineered ignorance' is not a safe harbor, but GDPR does not explicitly prohibit the design pattern either.

evidence.source: EDPB Statement 1/2025 on Age Assurance (adopted 11 Feb 2025)
evidence.finding: 10-principle statement explicitly states platforms 'should not rely on self-declaration as this is dependent on the goodwill of the user.' Requires risk-based, proportionate age assurance. No rebuttable-presumption framework endorsed. Strengthens the case that absence-of-verification is not a safe harbor — this is post-matrix new development.

evidence.source: FTC COPPA Rule / Federal Register (federalregister.gov, 2024 NPRM)
evidence.finding: FTC explicitly DECLINED to create a rebuttable presumption allowing general-audience platforms to presume users are adults. US 'actual knowledge' standard applies — the FTC has enforcement history but no rebuttable-presumption-from-account-existence doctrine. The claimed 'US 3b posture' framing overstates how defined the US standard is.

evidence.source: Datatilsynet website and Norwegian government sources (datatilsynet.no; regjeringen.no)
evidence.finding: No Datatilsynet guidance found on platform-side consent signals, cross-platform age signals, or engineered ignorance. Norway's government is proposing a social media age-verification bill (under-16) for 2026 Parliament introduction, and separately consulting on raising data consent age from 13 to 15. Datatilsynet has raised privacy concerns about mandatory age verification but issued no blessing or rejection of platform reliance on signals.

challenge.correctedStatement: Norway's "3b posture" on platform-side consent signals is genuine regulatory silence, not a doctrine. Datatilsynet has neither blessed nor rejected reliance on consent signals or "engineered ignorance," which is an arguable posture but not a safe harbor. Two corrections to the original framing: (1) the "rebuttable presumption from account existence / mirroring the US 3b" framing is a synthesis-layer construct found in no Norwegian or US primary source — the FTC affirmatively declined to create such a presumption (2024 NPRM), so the US parallel is an imprecise analogy, not a mirror; Norway is closer to pure silence than to a developed actual-knowledge doctrine. (2) Soft-law pressure exists but is modest and narrowly scoped: EDPB Statement 1/2025 (non-binding) disfavors self-declaration and pushes risk-based age assurance, and Norway's pending 13→15 consent-age raise (consultation closed Oct 2025, not enacted) plus DSA Art 28 (applies in Norway only from ~summer 2026) target ISS/social-media "online platforms" — a consumer AI tutor likely sits outside Art 28's scope. Net: avoidance designs face GDPR Art 8 "reasonable efforts" pressure but no prohibition; "arguable, not safe harbor" stands.
challenge.reasoning: The claim's operative conclusion — Datatilsynet silence is real, engineered ignorance is arguable but not a safe harbor — survives attack and is confirmed by primary sources. My research found nothing that overturns it; if anything the scope of EDPB/DSA/social-media pressure is narrower for a consumer AI tutor than the verifier implied, helping the "arguable" posture. The weak spot is the "rebuttable-presumption/mirrors-US-3b" framing, which no primary source supports (FTC declined it). That is a labeling flaw in the synthesis layer, not a substantive error in the conclusion — hence sustained with the verifier's corrections folded in, confidence trimmed for the imprecise analogy and the non-binding-but-enforcement-relevant EDPB caveat.

counterEvidence.source: EDPB Statement 1/2025 on Age Assurance (adopted 11 Feb 2025) — legal-force commentary (William Fry, RPC, Lexology)
counterEvidence.finding: Statement is non-binding general guidance, not binding guidelines. It disfavors self-declaration ('depends on goodwill of the user') and questions it in high-risk scenarios but does NOT prohibit the design. Supports 'arguable, not safe harbor' more than it refutes it.

originalClaim: France's Code de la consommation contains marketing-to-minors provisions, and CNIL is the French DPA; neither was researched in this corpus, so no FR-specific marketing restriction can be cited beyond the EU baseline.

verify.correction: The two factual sub-claims are correct: the Code de la consommation does contain marketing-to-minors provisions (specifically Art. L121-7(5) — direct incitement of children to purchase is a listed aggressive commercial practice — and Art. L121-6 on vulnerable groups), and CNIL is unambiguously France's DPA. However, the consequential conclusion — that "no FR-specific marketing restriction can be cited beyond the EU baseline" — is wrong. Art. L121-7(5) is citable FR national law (even if it implements Annex I(28) of the EU Unfair Commercial Practices Directive), and CNIL has published 8 child-specific digital-rights recommendations and enforces the 2024 SREN Law (No. 2024-449), both of which extend beyond bare GDPR requirements. The matrix should cite Art. L121-7(5) Code de la consommation and note CNIL's children's digital-rights recommendations as FR-specific authorities supplementing the EU baseline.
verify.notes: The claim is a self-aware admission of incomplete research ("neither was researched"). The two identity/existence sub-claims (Code contains provisions; CNIL is the DPA) are confirmed. The derived conclusion (no FR-specific citation possible beyond EU baseline) is the error: primary-source research shows citable FR provisions exist. Matrix confidence of 0.5 was appropriately cautious but the correct posture is to cite Art. L121-7(5) and CNIL children recommendations rather than defaulting to "only EU baseline."

evidence.source: Légifrance — Code de la consommation, Article L121-7(5)
evidence.finding: Article L121-7(5) explicitly prohibits directly inciting children to purchase or to persuade parents to buy advertised products. Article L121-6 also applies stricter unfair-practices assessment to vulnerable groups including children. These are enforceable FR provisions, not merely an EU baseline restatement.

evidence.source: CNIL official homepage — confirmed as France's GDPR supervisory authority/DPA
evidence.finding: CNIL (Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés) is confirmed as France's independent data protection supervisory authority under GDPR, established 1978. It chairs the June 2026 G7 DPA Roundtable in Paris. No ambiguity about its identity or role.

evidence.source: CNIL — Digital rights of children topic page
evidence.finding: CNIL has published 8 recommendations on children's digital rights, including age-appropriate consent, parental controls, and privacy-by-design obligations for services likely used by minors — going beyond the GDPR baseline in specificity.

evidence.source: France SREN Law No. 2024-449 of 21 May 2024 (via Baker McKenzie / ICLG reporting)
evidence.finding: France's SREN Law 2024-449 imposes FR-specific obligations on online services regarding minors, including age verification systems and local data-processing requirements — these are national rules beyond the EU GDPR/AVMSD baseline.

evidence.source: Légifrance — Code de la consommation full text (last modified 2026-05-28)
evidence.finding: The Code de la consommation was last updated 2026-05-28, confirming it is current and in force. It contains marketing-to-minors provisions in Book I, Title II (unfair commercial practices). The code is a live, citable FR-specific statute.

challenge.correctedStatement: The two factual sub-claims are confirmed: Code de la consommation Art. L121-7 item 5 lists, as an aggressive commercial practice, advertising that directly incites children to buy or to persuade parents to buy (with L121-6 adding a vulnerable-group test), and CNIL is unambiguously France's DPA. The claim's derived conclusion is overstated: a citable FR-codified provision (L121-7(5)) and CNIL's 8 children's-rights recommendations do exist and should be cited. However, the verifier overstates the reverse: L121-7(5) is a maximum-harmonisation verbatim transposition of UCPD Annex I(28) — substantively the EU baseline itself, just FR-codified and FR-enforced — not a stricter FR-specific marketing rule. SREN Law 2024-449 age-verification duties target pornographic platforms and likely do not reach a general AI-tutoring app. Correct posture: cite L121-7(5) and CNIL recommendations as FR-codified authorities, while noting the substantive standard equals the EU baseline.
challenge.reasoning: Primary sources confirm both factual sub-claims and the verifier's exact citations (Légifrance L121-7 item 5; CNIL DPA + 8 recs). The claim's research-gap framing survives, with the verifier's correction that citable FR provisions exist. My attack lands only at the margin: UCPD maximum-harmonisation means L121-7(5) is the EU baseline verbatim, so the verifier slightly overstates 'beyond baseline'; SREN's age-verification scope (porn sites) likely doesn't reach a tutoring app. Net: claim+correction sustained with caveats; strength weak.

originalClaim: Sweden's Marknadsföringslagen (Marketing Practices Act) and DPA IMY (Integritetsskyddsmyndigheten) were not researched in this corpus, so no SE-specific marketing-to-minors rule or IMY position can be cited beyond the EU baseline.

verify.correction: The corpus gap is real: Sweden and IMY appear not to have been researched in this matrix. However, the conclusion that therefore \"no SE-specific marketing-to-minors rule or IMY position can be cited beyond the EU baseline\" is misleading as a standalone claim, because such rules and positions exist and are readily citable. Correction: \"SE and IMY were not researched in this corpus. In fact, significant SE-specific rules do exist beyond the EU baseline: Marknadsföringslagen (SFS 2008:486) bans TV advertising targeting children under 12 and restricts direct marketing to under-16s without guardian consent; IMY's Bonnier News AB enforcement action (SEK 13M) cites unlawful behavioural profiling for ad targeting; IMY has designated children a formal 2026 supervision priority and published a dedicated digital-platform child-rights stakeholder guide. These must be added to the matrix as SE-specific obligations for a children-facing AI-tutoring product.\"
verify.notes: The claim is internally coherent as a self-description of corpus coverage (\"we didn't fetch these sources\"), but the implied downstream reading — that SE imposes nothing beyond EU baseline — is false and compliance-consequential for a minor-facing app. The matrix should add: (1) Marknadsföringslagen §§ on child advertising; (2) IMY's Bonnier News targeted-advertising enforcement; (3) IMY 2026 children priority; (4) Swedish DPA Act Ch. 2 §4 (age 13 consent floor). Confidence 0.82 because native-language statute text was not fully parsed (government.se PDF returned 403).

evidence.source: Marknadsföringslag (2008:486) — riksdagen.se / government.se
evidence.finding: Marknadsföringslagen SFS 2008:486 contains SE-specific child advertising rules that materially exceed the EU UCPD baseline: TV advertising aimed at children under 12 is banned; direct marketing to under-16s requires guardian consent; direct exhortation to children to buy or pressure parents is prohibited.

evidence.source: IMY 2026 Priorities — imy.se/en
evidence.finding: IMY named children and young people as a formal 2026 supervision priority, with stated focus on increasing awareness of minors' personal data rights online. This constitutes a citable IMY position on child data protection, though not a formal enforcement guideline on marketing.

evidence.source: Chambers Data Protection & Privacy Guide 2026 — Sweden chapter
evidence.finding: Swedish Data Protection Act Ch. 2 §4 lowers GDPR Article 8 consent age to 13. IMY's Bonnier News AB decision (SEK 13 million fine) found unlawful behavioural profiling for targeted advertising — a citable IMY position directly on marketing and personal data.

evidence.source: IMY Children on Digital Platforms report — imy.se
evidence.finding: IMY published a dedicated stakeholder guide on rights of children and young people on digital platforms — a citable, SE-DPA-authored position on child data protection that goes beyond a bare EU-baseline restatement.

evidence.source: CE Sweden / Lexology — Marknadsföringslagen analysis
evidence.finding: Secondary legal commentary confirms SE-specific child advertising prohibitions under Marknadsföringslagen: TV ad ban under 12; exploitation of children's credulity prohibited; indirect persuasion (pressuring parents) prohibited — all enforceable by Konsumentverket and Patent and Market Court.

challenge.correctedStatement: SE and IMY were not researched in this corpus (uncontested). SE-specific rules DO exist beyond the EU baseline, but with corrections to the verifier's anchors: (1) The total ban on TV advertising aimed at children under 12 lives in the Radio and Television Act (radio- och tv-lagen 2010:696, Ch. 8 §§7-9), NOT Marknadsföringslagen 2008:486; it is broadcast-scoped and does not directly reach a consumer AI-tutoring app. (2) Marknadsföringslagen's child rules (direct-exhortation / credulity-exploitation bans, §7) largely transpose the UCPD Annex I blacklist — close to the EU baseline, not gold-plating. (3) IMY's Bonnier News decision (SEK 13M, upheld by Stockholm Administrative Court) is a general adult behavioural-profiling case (Art 6(1) consent vs legitimate interest), NOT a minors-specific case. (4) "Children/young people" is a genuine IMY 2026 supervision priority (education/awareness-focused; the AI strand is public-sector-scoped). (5) Swedish DPA Act Ch. 2 §4 sets the Art 8 consent floor at 13. Net: SE imposes real child-protection obligations, but the directly app-relevant ones flow mainly from GDPR-as-applied (IMY's child guidance, the 13 consent floor), not a unique marketing-to-minors statute reaching this product.
challenge.reasoning: Corpus gap (claim's factual core) is uncontested and the verifier's direction is right: SE rules exist. But the verifier's anchors have material errors. Primary statute text shows the flagship under-12 TV ad ban is in the Radio and Television Act 2010:696 Ch.8, not Marknadsföringslagen 2008:486 — wrong-statute attribution. That ban is broadcast-scoped, not reaching a consumer app. Marknadsföringslagen's child rules largely track the UCPD baseline. Bonnier News (SEK 13M, amount correct) is an adult-profiling case, not minors. IMY child priority is real but education-scoped; AI strand is public-sector. Direction survives; citations need re-anchoring.

originalClaim: Denmark's Markedsføringsloven (distinct from the Norwegian act) and the Danish DPA (Datatilsynet) were not researched in this corpus, so no DK-specific marketing-to-minors rule can be cited beyond the EU baseline.

verify.correction: The claim is refuted. Denmark's Markedsføringsloven § 11 is a well-established, publicly accessible statute containing DK-specific marketing-to-minors rules that demonstrably exceed the EU UCPD baseline: a blanket prohibition on any alcohol reference in marketing directed at under-18s; a prohibition on marketing on or via social media profiles of under-15s; a prohibition on featuring under-15s in social media marketing (with a narrow natural-illustration exception). The Consumer Ombudsman (Forbrugerombudsmanden) enforces these actively and updated guidance as recently as February 2024. Danish Datatilsynet also has a distinct, active role in children's digital-data protection (Oslo Declaration, May 2024; AI DPIA templates). The corpus gap is real — these sources were not fetched — but the conclusion drawn from the gap ("no DK-specific rule can be cited") is wrong. The correct statement is: Denmark has DK-specific marketing-to-minors rules in Markedsføringsloven § 11 (stk. 1–4) that the research corpus did not cover; the matrix cell should be populated using forbrugerombudsmanden.dk and retsinformation.dk LBK nr 1420/2024.
verify.notes: The matrix correctly notes the corpus gap but incorrectly equates "not in corpus" with "cannot be cited." Markedsføringsloven § 11 is a primary statute citable right now. The digital-consent age for DK is 15 (trusted finding #1, LOV nr 1783 af 28/12/2023) — relevant context for an AI-tutoring app targeting minors. The matrix should add a DK row covering § 11 (marketing) and Datatilsynet children guidance alongside the consent-age correction.

evidence.source: Forbrugerombudsmanden (Danish Consumer Ombudsman) — Markedsføring rettet mod børn og unge
evidence.finding: Markedsføringsloven § 11 contains multiple DK-specific rules beyond EU UCPD baseline: blanket ban on alcohol references in any marketing to under-18s; prohibition on marketing on or via social media profiles belonging to or appearing to belong to under-15s; ban on using under-15s in social media marketing except natural product illustration.

evidence.source: danskelove.dk — Markedsføringsloven § 11
evidence.finding: § 11 stk. 1–4 codifies the under-18 violence/danger prohibition, substance-reference prohibition, and the under-15 social-media prohibition. These are Danish domestic provisions supplementing the EU Unfair Commercial Practices Directive, not mere transpositions of EU baseline.

evidence.source: retsinformation.dk — Markedsføringsloven LBK nr 1420 (2024)
evidence.finding: Consolidated 2024 version of the Marketing Practices Act confirmed as current law (2024 consolidation). § 11 amendments in 2021 (LOV 2021:2192) expanded protections for minors specifically on social media. Access required authentication on direct fetch but confirmed via retsinformation search.

evidence.source: Datatilsynet (Danish DPA) — English homepage and Oslo Declaration (May 2024)
evidence.finding: Danish Datatilsynet actively supervises children's data protection, led Nordic Oslo Declaration on children in online gaming (May 2024), published AI DPIA templates. Datatilsynet is a distinct authority from the Norwegian Datatilsynet — both use the same name but are separate bodies.

evidence.source: Dansk Erhverv — Reglerne for markedsføring rettet mod børn ændres (Feb 2024)
evidence.finding: Consumer Ombudsman updated guidance on marketing-to-minors rules in February 2024, confirming the rules are actively enforced. Updated guidance addresses influencer classification and advertising disclosure standards specifically for child audiences.

challenge.correctedStatement: The original claim is refuted on its conclusion, sustained only on its factual premise. True: DK Markedsføringsloven and Datatilsynet were not in this research corpus. Wrong: the inference that 'no DK-specific marketing-to-minors rule can be cited.' Markedsføringsloven § 11 (LBK nr 1420 af 02/12/2024; children's rules added by LOV nr 2192 af 30/11/2021, in force 28 May 2022) contains DK-specific provisions exceeding the EU UCPD baseline — notably stk. 3's prohibition on commercial practices via under-15s' social-media profiles and on using under-15s in social-media marketing (no UCPD analogue), plus stk. 2's ban on intoxicant/alcohol references in marketing directed at under-18s and stk. 1's violence/danger ban. Datatilsynet (DK) is a distinct, active children's-data authority (Oslo Declaration May 2024; AI DPIA templates 22 May 2024). Minor: 'blanket alcohol' framing slightly overstates stk. 2, which is context-bound to under-18-directed marketing. Note: DK digital-consent age is 15.

Only crack: verifier's 'blanket alcohol' wording mildly overstates stk. 2's context-bound scope — non-fatal. Verifier's refuted verdict and correction survive.

judge.finalStatement: Denmark has DK-specific marketing-to-minors rules beyond the EU UCPD baseline that the research corpus did not fetch but that are publicly citable now: Markedsføringsloven § 11 (current consolidation LBK nr 1420 af 02/12/2024; children's rules added by LOV nr 2192 af 30/11/2021, in force from 1 Jan 2022). Specifically: stk. 1 bans marketing to under-18s that encourages violence/dangerous conduct or exploits fear/superstition; stk. 2 bans references to intoxicants incl. alcohol (and other under-18-unsuitable products) in marketing directed at under-18s; stk. 3 prohibits commercial practices on/via social-media profiles belonging to (or appearing to belong to) under-15s and prohibits using under-15s in such marketing absent a natural product-illustration context (no UCPD analogue); stk. 4 grants ministerial rulemaking power. Enforced by Forbrugerombudsmanden (Consumer Ombudsman). The Danish Datatilsynet is a distinct, active children's-data authority (Nordic Oslo Declaration, May 2024; AI/general DPIA templates). Populate the DK matrix cell from forbrugerombudsmanden.dk and retsinformation.dk LBK nr 1420/2024 — the claim's conclusion ("no DK-specific rule can be cited") is wrong, even though the corpus-gap premise is true.
judge.reasoning: Decisive question: does DK Markedsføringsloven § 11 carry DK-specific minor-marketing rules exceeding EU UCPD? Primary text (danskelove.dk § 11; Forbrugerombudsmanden guidance; retsinformation.dk LBK 1420/2024) confirms stk. 1-4 verbatim, incl. the under-15 social-media-profile ban with no UCPD analogue. The claim's conclusion is refuted; only its corpus-gap premise holds. Verifier's verdict and correction are correct; the challenger's "weak/sustained" label is mislabeling — it agrees the conclusion is refuted, differing only on the minor "blanket alcohol" overstatement, which does not change the matrix outcome.

originalClaim: Estonia is active in EU AI Act regulatory-sandbox pilots, but no specific EE-published position on AI-for-minors has surfaced in the corpus.

verify.correction: The second half of the claim — no EE-published position on AI-for-minors has surfaced — is accurate. The first half is overstated: Estonia is not confirmed as \"active\" in EU AI Act Art. 57 regulatory-sandbox pilots. As of April 2026, only Spain had an operational sandbox; Estonia's Art. 57 sandbox status had not been communicated. Estonia's strategy documents reference internal \"AI sandboxes\" in a general sense, but these predate and are distinct from the formal EU AI Act regulatory-sandbox framework. A corrected statement: Estonia references AI sandboxes in its 2024–2030 strategy but has not confirmed active participation in EU AI Act Art. 57 regulatory-sandbox pilots as of mid-2026, and no specific EE-published position on AI-for-minors has surfaced.
verify.notes: The claim conflates Estonia's general AI-strategy sandbox language with confirmed active Art. 57 pilot participation. The EP Think Tank April 2026 report is the most current primary source and does not name Estonia as active. The matrix should lower confidence on the sandbox-activity assertion and flag it as unconfirmed rather than active. The no-EE-position-on-AI-for-minors sub-claim remains accurate as of June 2026. Original confidence of 0.65 appears slightly high for the sandbox-active component.

evidence.source: European Parliament Think Tank, April 2026
evidence.finding: As of April 2026, only Spain had an operational EU AI Act regulatory sandbox. Five additional states were actively implementing; 16 had not yet communicated plans. Estonia is not named as active. The August 2, 2026 deadline had not yet been met by most states.

evidence.source: artificialintelligenceact.eu — EU Member State Sandbox Overview
evidence.finding: This tracker of member-state AI sandbox approaches does not specifically list Estonia as having an active or planned Art. 57 sandbox. The page is noted as a work in progress, but Estonia's absence from named active states is notable.

evidence.source: Estonia Data and AI White Paper 2024–2030 / Kratt Action Plan 2024–2026
evidence.finding: Estonia's strategy references internal 'AI sandboxes' for public-sector testing, but these are strategic/policy constructs predating the AI Act's Art. 57 formal sandbox requirement — not confirmed as Art. 57 regulatory-sandbox pilots.

evidence.source: Global Legal Insights – AI Laws Estonia 2025 / search sweep
evidence.finding: No EE-published official regulatory position on AI specifically for minors was located. Estonia's 'AI Leap' initiative (2025) is an education procurement programme, not a regulatory position. Absence is confirmed across multiple searches.

evidence.source: EU AI Act Article 57 (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689)
evidence.finding: Member states must establish at least one operational AI regulatory sandbox by 2 August 2026. The obligation can also be met by participating in an existing sandbox. Estonia has not been confirmed as meeting this obligation early or as running a pilot sandbox before the deadline.

challenge.correctedStatement: Estonia references AI sandboxes in its Kratt Action Plan 2024-2026 and plans an AI Competence Centre to manage them, but has NOT been confirmed as running an active EU AI Act Art. 57 regulatory-sandbox pilot. As of June 2026, only Spain (one tracker says Denmark) is named operational; Estonia appears in no primary tracker's active list. Note: the Art. 57 national-sandbox deadline was postponed from 2 Aug 2026 to 2 Aug 2027 by the Digital Omnibus (Council-confirmed 13 May 2026). No specific EE-published position on AI-for-minors has surfaced.

These weaken the verifier's framing, not its conclusion. Net: sustained with a deadline caveat; confidence raised from verifier's 0.72.

originalClaim: The Google Gemini API terms prohibit use for services directed at / with an under-18 audience; this prohibition does NOT bind when the controller has no actual knowledge of an under-age user, but binds (becoming a Terms-of-Service breach — synthesis Gap A) once the controller knows the user is under-age.

verify.correction: The first half of the claim is confirmed: Google Gemini API terms do prohibit API clients directed toward or likely accessed by under-18 users. The second half is refuted: the terms contain NO actual-knowledge carve-out. The prohibition is framed as an objective test — 'directed towards or is likely to be accessed by' individuals under 18 — and applies regardless of whether the controller has actual knowledge of any individual user's age. The 'synthesis Gap A' characterisation (binds only on actual knowledge) has no foundation in the primary terms text. A controller cannot rely on ignorance of a specific user's age as a defence; the test is whether the service is designed for or likely to attract under-18s.
verify.notes: The matrix's 'synthesis Gap A' framing is the error: it imports an actual-knowledge standard that does not exist in the Gemini API ToS. The ToS uses a structural/design test ('directed towards or likely accessed by'), not a per-user knowledge test. The matrix should delete the 'does NOT bind without actual knowledge' clause and replace it with: the prohibition is objective — it triggers on service design/audience, not controller knowledge of any individual user's age. Confidence 0.97 reflects direct primary-source confirmation; the 0.03 residual covers the possibility of a separate Google addendum or programme-specific supplement not surfaced by this fetch.

evidence.source: Google Gemini API Additional Terms of Service (current, last updated 2026-04-28)
evidence.finding: Age Requirements section states verbatim: 'You must be 18 years of age or older to use the APIs. You also will not use the Services as part of a website, application, or other service (collectively, "API Clients") that is directed towards or is likely to be accessed by individuals under the age of 18.' No actual-knowledge exception exists.

evidence.source: Gemini API Additional Terms of Service Archive (2024-12-20 version)
evidence.finding: The archived December 2024 version contains identical age-restriction language, confirming the 'directed towards or likely to be accessed' objective standard has been stable across versions — no actual-knowledge carve-out in any version.

evidence.source: Trusted Prior Finding #2 (verified 2026-06-04/05)
evidence.finding: Prior verification confirmed: Google Gemini API / Google Cloud generative-AI DEFAULT terms restrict customer API clients directed toward or likely accessed by under-18s — distinct from Google-operated Gemini Apps / Workspace for Education routes. Corroborates primary source; no knowledge exception noted.

challenge.correctedStatement: The Google Gemini API Additional Terms (effective March 23, 2026; identical language since at least Dec 2024) require all API users to be 18+ AND prohibit using the API in any service 'directed towards or likely to be accessed by individuals under the age of 18.' This is an objective design/audience test that binds regardless of the controller's actual knowledge of any individual user's age. There is NO actual-knowledge carve-out; the 'synthesis Gap A' framing (prohibition triggers only once the controller knows a user is under-age) has no basis in the terms. For an AI-tutoring app likely accessed by minors, the prohibition binds at the default-Gemini-API level irrespective of per-user age knowledge.
challenge.reasoning: Attacked the verification by hunting for a knowledge exception, an enterprise/Vertex carve-out, or a newer term version — none exists. Current (Mar 23, 2026) and archived (Dec 2024) primary texts both use the objective 'directed towards or likely accessed by under-18' test with zero actual-knowledge language; a Google forum confirms it applies via Vertex too. The claim's load-bearing second half (binds only on actual knowledge) is fatally wrong; verifier's correction holds. Minor verifier slip: cited update date 2026-04-28 vs live effective date 2026-03-23 — immaterial. 0.03 residual covers unsurfaced program-specific addenda.

counterEvidence.finding: Age section verbatim: must be 18+ to use APIs, and will not use Services in an API Client 'directed towards or is likely to be accessed by individuals under the age of 18.' No 'actual knowledge'/'knowingly' carve-out anywhere. Objective design/audience test.

counterEvidence.finding: Identical age language and identical absence of any actual-knowledge exception. Confirms the objective standard is stable across versions; the claim's knowledge-gated framing was never in any version.

originalClaim: Anthropic has a CSAM-reporting commitment (per its AUP) that applies globally as a platform term and binds regardless of consent.

verify.notes: The claim uses "regardless of consent" — the AUP's exact phrase is "regardless of jurisdiction" in its minor definition, which achieves the same legal effect (local age-of-consent laws create no exception). This is accurate characterization, not a verbatim quote. NCMEC (US-based) is the named reporting authority; the AUP does not name international equivalents, though the reporting commitment is framed globally. The detection mechanism (hash-matching) operates on first-party services; API operator-side detection is not mandated, though operators are contractually prohibited from permitting CSAM use.

evidence.source: Anthropic AUP (anthropic.com/legal/aup) — primary
evidence.finding: AUP explicitly states: 'When we detect CSAM (including AI-generated CSAM)...we will report to relevant authorities.' Minor defined as 'any individual under the age of 18 years old, regardless of jurisdiction' — no consent-based carve-out. Applies to all users 'including via any authorized resellers or passthrough access.'

evidence.source: Anthropic AUP — Universal Usage Standards classification
evidence.finding: Child-safety prohibitions (CSAM creation/distribution, minor grooming, sexualization of minors) are listed under 'Universal Usage Standards' — the AUP's non-waivable tier. No operator permission override is available for these provisions.

evidence.source: Anthropic support.claude.com article 9020328 — CSAM Detection and Reporting
evidence.finding: Confirms hash-matching detection on first-party services; NCMEC is the designated reporting authority. Policy presented without jurisdictional carve-outs. Applies to user and organization inputs. No consent exception mentioned.

evidence.source: Anthropic Child Safety Principles (anthropic.com/news/child-safety-principles)
evidence.finding: Commits to reporting detected CSAM to NCMEC using the Generative AI File Annotation; explicitly prohibits customer use of models 'to further sexual harms against children,' confirming downstream operator binding via contractual restriction.

evidence.source: Anthropic Child Safety Progress Report, May 2025 (PDF)
evidence.finding: Confirms CSAM reporting obligations as Anthropic-wide commitments across products and training data. Aligns with Thorn standards. No geographic limitation noted. Consistent with AUP's universal framing.

final.statement: Anthropic has a CSAM-reporting commitment (per its AUP) that applies globally as a platform term and binds regardless of consent.
final.reasoning: The claim uses "regardless of consent" — the AUP's exact phrase is "regardless of jurisdiction" in its minor definition, which achieves the same legal effect (local age-of-consent laws create no exception). This is accurate characterization, not a verbatim quote. NCMEC (US-based) is the named reporting authority; the AUP does not name international equivalents, though the reporting commitment is framed globally. originalClaim: Apple Family Sharing and Google Family Link are platform-side account mechanisms (their mechanics verified) but neither functions as a regulator-blessed consent signal; the EDPB §3 analysis would likely treat a parent holding an Apple ID as insufficient.

verify.correction: The claim is largely accurate but overstates one thing and undersupports another. The mechanics-verified characterization is correct: Apple Family Sharing and Google Family Link function as platform-internal parental consent systems, explicitly scoped to Apple's/Google's own services. Apple's own legal disclosure confirms third-party app developers must obtain separate verifiable parental consent — i.e., Family Sharing is not a usable consent signal for a third-party app. The claim that neither is "regulator-blessed" as a consent signal is also correct: no EDPB, CNIL, or ICO document endorses either mechanism as a valid GDPR Article 8 parental consent method for third-party services. However, the specific framing "EDPB §3 analysis would likely treat a parent holding an Apple ID as insufficient" is an inference rather than a documented EDPB position. The EDPB has not published guidance that specifically evaluates Apple Family Sharing or Google Family Link against Article 8. The more accurate statement is: (a) GDPR Article 8(2) requires reasonable efforts to verify the consenting person actually holds parental responsibility — a condition that mere account co-membership does not satisfy on its own; (b) EDPB Statement 1/2025 sets principles (robustness, accuracy, data minimisation) that platform account existence alone cannot meet; (c) Apple itself excludes third-party apps from its consent scope. The "§3" reference in the claim is unverifiable — no EDPB document §3 addressing this specific scenario was located. The claim should be read as: Apple/Google platform accounts are platform-internal mechanisms inapplicable to third-party services as consent signals; EDPB and member-state guidance (CNIL) do not treat account-existence as sufficient parental consent verification.
verify.notes: The "EDPB §3 analysis" framing is the weak link: no EDPB document section 3 directly analyzing Apple Family Sharing or Google Family Link as GDPR Article 8 consent signals was located. The underlying proposition — that neither mechanism satisfies Article 8 verification requirements for third-party services — is well-supported by Apple's own legal disclosure plus EDPB/CNIL principles-based guidance, but it rests on inference from first principles, not a named EDPB ruling. Matrix should flag this as "supported by inference from primary sources, not a direct EDPB ruling."

evidence.source: Apple Family Privacy Disclosure (apple.com/legal/privacy/en-ww/parent-disclosure/)
evidence.finding: Apple explicitly states its Family Sharing parental consent applies only to Apple's own services and data collection. It DOES NOT apply to third-party app developers, who must obtain separate verifiable parental consent. COPPA is cited; GDPR is not mentioned. This confirms the 'not a regulator-blessed third-party consent signal' proposition.

evidence.source: GDPR Article 8(2) — reasonable efforts standard
evidence.finding: Article 8(2) requires controllers to make 'reasonable efforts to verify that consent is given or authorised by the holder of parental responsibility.' The threshold is risk-calibrated: email verification may suffice for low-risk; higher-risk processing requires stronger verification. Platform account existence alone is not stated to be sufficient.

evidence.source: EDPB Statement 1/2025 on Age Assurance (edpb.europa.eu, February 2025)
evidence.finding: EDPB sets ten principles for GDPR-compliant age assurance (data minimization, proportionality, robustness, etc.) but does NOT endorse or reject specific platform mechanisms such as Apple Family Sharing or Google Family Link. No 'regulator-blessed' list of approved consent signals is published.

evidence.source: CNIL Recommendation 7 on age and parental consent (cnil.fr)
evidence.finding: CNIL does not evaluate or endorse Apple Family Sharing or Google Family Link as valid consent mechanisms. It calls for trusted-third-party verifiers with actual identity/parental-status checks, and notes that overly simple mechanisms are insufficient. European Commission is expected to develop suitable infrastructure.

evidence.source: Apple Support article 102033 — Family Sharing parental consent flow
evidence.finding: Apple's own support page describes Family Sharing as its internal mechanism for child Apple Account setup. The page does not assert GDPR compliance nor claim the mechanism satisfies external regulatory requirements for third-party services.

originalClaim: Cross-cutting interpretive principle: consent (parental or self) cannot unlock AI Act Art 5 prohibitions, OpenAI Root-level U18 protections, the Gemini under-18 prohibition, or the Anthropic CSAM-reporting commitment — these are prohibition-floors distinct from the consent-edge primitive, requiring a separate data-model primitive.

The interpretive design inference — that a data model requires a separate primitive for these floors vs. the consent-edge primitive — is a sound engineering conclusion that follows from verified legal facts, not a separate factual claim requiring independent verification. Confidence docked slightly from 0.92 because the claim bundles four distinct provider policies into one assertion and the Anthropic article 9307344 (support.claude.com) specifically did not surface CSAM-reporting language in the fetched content, though the AUP at anthropic.com/legal/aup confirmed it directly.

originalClaim: Synthesis Gap C: crisis-escalation in the product is prompt-only — there is no structural escalation channel, no guardian notification, and no human-in-the-loop hold; consent does not make prompt-only crisis handling sufficient, and AI Act plus platform terms arguably require structural routing.

verify.correction: The core concern is legitimate: prompt-only crisis handling for an AI app serving minors is a real gap, and consent alone does not satisfy best-practice or emerging legal standards. However, the claim overstates the current binding legal position. (1) The AI Act does not clearly classify a general AI tutoring assistant as high-risk under Annex III, so the Article 9 risk-management obligations do not automatically require structural escalation channels — and the high-risk provisions are not yet in force until 2 August 2026. (2) Anthropic and OpenAI platform guidelines recommend escalation paths but use advisory, not mandatory, language; they impose no specific structural requirement on deployers. (3) Google Gemini API terms prohibit under-18 use entirely rather than requiring escalation structures. (4) No current binding EU or EEA instrument explicitly mandates structural crisis-escalation channels or guardian notification for AI tutoring apps as of 2026-06-05. The gap is real and the normative argument is sound, but the claim that AI Act plus platform terms 'arguably require' structural routing is an interpretive stretch beyond what the primary sources currently say. Correct framing: prompt-only crisis handling is a significant unmitigated risk; Anthropic guidelines strongly recommend structural escalation; California SB 243 (US/CA only) requires crisis helpline routing; but no currently binding instrument explicitly mandates structural human-in-the-loop or guardian notification for a tutoring app as of today.
verify.notes: The claim conflates two things: (a) the normative adequacy of prompt-only handling (where the concern is well-founded), and (b) a binding legal requirement from AI Act + platform terms (where the evidence is weaker). AI Act Annex III likely does not cover general tutoring AI, making the AI Act hook uncertain. Platform terms are advisory on crisis escalation. The 0.85 file confidence is too high for a claim that rests on an interpretive extension of sources that don't directly mandate structural escalation for tutoring apps. California SB 243 is the closest enacted instrument but is US/CA-only and does not require guardian notification or human review.

challenge.correctedStatement: Synthesis Gap C is real and the verifier's "partially_correct" holds, but with two corrections. (2) The platform-terms hook is STRONGER than the verifier said: OpenAI's under-18 API guidance lists "escalation paths for high-risk interactions" under a "Safety Standards / must take reasonable steps" obligation for organizations serving minors — contractually-adjacent, not purely advisory. Correct framing: prompt-only crisis handling for a minors-accessible AI tutor is a genuine unmitigated gap; consent does not cure it; OpenAI's deployer terms and Anthropic's minors guidelines push monitoring/escalation; DSA Art 28 (in force since Feb 2024, broad "accessible to minors" reading) pushes helpline access but likely exempts a micro/small-enterprise app and its guidelines are non-binding. No instrument as of 2026-06-05 binds GUARDIAN NOTIFICATION on a tutoring deployer — that remains best-practice only (OpenAI offers it for consumer ChatGPT, not via API terms).

originalClaim: For Estonia (an EU member state), the matrix cites DSA Art 28 profiling-based-advertising ban for minors as applicable but flags it as unverified in the corpus; in the EE profiling cell it sits alongside the verified GDPR Art 22/Recital 71 and AI Act Annex III framework.

verify.correction: The claim that DSA Art 28 is 'applicable' to an AI tutoring app in Estonia (or any EU jurisdiction) is a scope error. Art 28 applies only to 'online platforms' — services that publicly disseminate user-generated content to a potentially unlimited audience (DSA Art 3(i)). A private AI tutoring app without public UGC dissemination almost certainly does not qualify as an 'online platform' under the DSA, so Art 28's profiling-based-advertising ban would not apply to it. The provision itself (the prohibition on profiling-based ads targeting known minors) is correctly described and is in force across all EU member states including Estonia since 17 February 2024. The pairing with GDPR Art 22/Recital 71 is conceptually coherent (both address profiling and minors) but these are legally distinct obligations. The matrix should flag Art 28 as 'not applicable — product likely outside DSA online-platform definition' rather than 'applicable but unverified.'
verify.notes: The 0.6 file confidence is appropriate given the unverified flag. The core claim about what Art 28 says is accurate. The main error is treating Art 28 as an EU-wide profiling rule for any minor-facing service — it is scoped to 'online platforms' only. GDPR Art 22 (automated decision-making) is the more likely applicable profiling provision for a tutoring app. The matrix should also note Art 28 is paragraph 2 specifically.

evidence.source: EUR-Lex / DSA Regulation (EU) 2022/2065, Article 28(2)
evidence.finding: Article 28(2) DSA prohibits presenting profiling-based advertisements (per GDPR Art 4(4) definition) to recipients known with reasonable certainty to be minors. This is the correct article for the described prohibition. Recital 71 is the corresponding recital, not a standalone source.

A private AI tutoring app (no public UGC dissemination) likely does not qualify as an online platform, making Art 28 inapplicable to this matrix's subject product.

I located the exact cell (data.json:1644-1655) and confirmed every element verbatim: Art 22/Recital 71 verified, Annex III verified, DSA Art 28 flagged unverified, exact citation_slug. The claim is descriptively accurate, so it survives. The verifier's scope correction (Art 28 reaches only "online platforms" under Art 3(i); a private tutoring app is likely outside) is legally sound and a worthwhile matrix caveat, but it corrects a strawman of the claim rather than the claim itself. Hence sustained, with the scope point folded in as a material improvement.

originalClaim: AI Act high-risk classification (Annex III §3) imposes record-keeping requirements on retention when the product is high-risk-classified, applied in the known-under retention cells across DE, FR, SE, DK, EE and (via EEA incorporation) NO, irrespective of age.

verify.correction: The core proposition is sound: Annex III §3 does provide the high-risk classification basis, and that classification triggers record-keeping obligations (log retention ≥6 months under Art. 19; technical documentation 10 years under Art. 18) that apply irrespective of user age, across all EU/EEA Member States including DE, FR, SE, DK, EE and NO. Two corrections are needed: (1) Source precision — Annex III itself only provides the classification; the retention obligations are imposed by Articles 12, 18, and 19, not by Annex III. The matrix should cite Art. 18 and Art. 19 as the retention sources, with Annex III §3 as only the classification anchor. (2) Applicability timing — as of 2026-06-05, the Digital Omnibus political deal (7 May 2026) has deferred Annex III stand-alone high-risk compliance from 2 August 2026 to 2 December 2027 (formal OJ adoption pending). The obligations are not yet in force for education AI systems; framing them as currently "applied" in retention cells is premature. The matrix should note: "Annex III §3 high-risk classification confirmed; record-keeping obligations (Art. 18: 10-yr tech docs; Art. 19: ≥6-month logs) apply irrespective of user age across all EEA states, but compliance date deferred to 2 December 2027 under Digital Omnibus (pending OJ publication)."
verify.notes: The matrix conflates the classification source (Annex III) with the retention-obligation source (Art. 18/19). These are distinct articles. Also, the Digital Omnibus deferral to Dec 2027 is a material status change since the matrix was authored — the "applied" framing needs a compliance-date caveat. The "irrespective of age" element is correct and well-supported. EEA incorporation of AI Act for NO is standard EEA Agreement process; no evidence of delay specific to AI Act as of this date.

evidence.source: EU AI Act Annex III (Regulation 2024/1689) — artificialintelligenceact.eu/annex/3/
evidence.finding: Annex III §3 correctly classifies four education/vocational-training AI use-cases as high-risk: admission/access decisions, learning-outcome evaluation, appropriate-level assessment, and prohibited-behaviour monitoring during tests. Applies at all educational levels, with no age-of-user carve-out.

evidence.source: EU AI Act Articles 12, 18, 19 — artificialintelligenceact.eu/article/12/, /article/18/, /article/19/
evidence.finding: Record-keeping obligations for high-risk AI flow from Articles 12/19 (automatic logs, minimum 6-month retention) and Article 18 (technical documentation, 10-year retention post-market). The cited source (Annex III) defines the classification but does not itself impose retention; the claim's source citation is technically incomplete.

evidence.source: EU AI Act Article 6(2) and Chapter III, Section 3 — artificialintelligenceact.eu
evidence.finding: High-risk obligations including record-keeping apply to any provider/deployer of an Annex III §3 system irrespective of user age. Age of users is not a criterion for classification or for the record-keeping duty — confirming the 'irrespective of age' element of the claim.

evidence.source: Digital Omnibus provisional agreement, May 7 2026 — Orrick / Gibson Dunn analysis
evidence.finding: Political deal of 7 May 2026 defers Annex III stand-alone high-risk compliance (including Articles 12, 18, 19 record-keeping) from 2 August 2026 to 2 December 2027. Formal adoption and OJ publication pending as of 2026-06-05 but expected before August 2026.

evidence.source: Gibson Dunn: EU AI Act Omnibus Agreement — Postponed High-Risk Deadlines
evidence.finding: Omnibus changes 'only take legal effect upon formal adoption and publication in the Official Journal.' The current date (2026-06-05) places Annex III obligations in the window between political agreement and formal OJ publication — meaning full enforcement is imminent but not yet binding.

challenge.correctedStatement: IF — and only if — the AI tutoring product is classified high-risk under AI Act Annex III §3, record-keeping obligations apply irrespective of user age across all EU/EEA states (DE, FR, SE, DK, EE, and NO via EEA incorporation). But two threshold defects must be resolved first: (1) Annex III §3 (a)-(d) all require an educational/vocational-training-institution context tied to admission, learning-outcome evaluation, level-assignment, or test-monitoring — a B2C consumer homework-helper with no grades/institution/admission role likely falls OUTSIDE §3, in which case it is not high-risk and no record-keeping attaches; (2) even if arguably in-scope, the Art. 6(3) filter can exempt a system performing a narrow procedural task that does not materially influence outcomes (though profiling of users would defeat that exemption). On sourcing: the retention duties are imposed by Articles 12/18/19 (logs ≥6 months; technical documentation 10 years) — Annex III §3 is only the classification anchor. On timing: as of 2026-06-05 the Digital Omnibus (political deal 7 May 2026) defers Annex III stand-alone high-risk compliance to 2 December 2027, binding only upon OJ publication (pending); Norway's AI Act is not yet in force (expected late summer 2026). The cell's unconditional present-tense 'applied... irrespective of age' framing is therefore premature both on the unestablished classification premise and on the deferred/not-yet-in-force timing.
challenge.reasoning: Verifier's age-neutrality, article-sourcing, and Omnibus-timing corrections are sound, but it skipped the threshold question: is a B2C consumer tutoring app even within Annex III §3? Points (a)-(d) all presuppose an educational-institution context plus admission/grading/monitoring functions — a homework-helper likely sits outside, defeating high-risk classification and the record-keeping duty. Art.6(3)'s narrow-task filter is a second off-ramp. Both the scope and the deferred/not-yet-in-force timing make the unconditional 'applied irrespective of age' framing overstated. The conditional core (IF high-risk, THEN age-neutral retention) survives.

counterEvidence.source: EU AI Act Annex III §3 (Regulation 2024/1689) — artificialintelligenceact.eu/annex/3/
counterEvidence.finding: All four sub-points (a)-(d) require AI used 'in the context of or within educational and vocational training institutions' for admission, learning-outcome evaluation, level-assignment, or test-monitoring. A B2C consumer homework-helper with no grades, no institution, no admission role likely falls OUTSIDE §3 — so not high-risk, no Art.18/19 record-keeping attaches. Verifier never tested in-scope threshold.

counterEvidence.source: EU AI Act Article 6(3) filter — artificialintelligenceact.eu/article/6/
counterEvidence.finding: Even an arguably-in-scope Annex III system is NOT high-risk if it poses no significant risk and performs a narrow procedural task or only improves a completed human activity. A tutor that explains without materially influencing outcomes may qualify out. Profiling of natural persons defeats the exemption — but only if §3-in-scope first.

counterEvidence.source: Gibson Dunn — EU AI Act Omnibus, Postponed High-Risk Deadlines (May 27 2026)
counterEvidence.finding: Annex III high-risk compliance (incl. Art.12/18/19 record-keeping) deferred to 2 Dec 2027, but only POLITICALLY agreed as of June 2026; 'the new dates do not bind until formal adoption and Official Journal publication.' Present-tense 'applied' framing in the cell is premature.

counterEvidence.source: AI laws in Norway — CMS / artificialintelligenceact.eu national plans
counterEvidence.finding: AI Act not yet incorporated via EEA Joint Committee as of June 2026; Norway's national AI Act scheduled to enter into force 'late summer 2026' (Nkom as coordinator). So the NO cell's present-tense 'applied irrespective of age' fails on timing for Norway specifically, separate from the substantive scope defect.

counterEvidence.source: EU AI Act Articles 18, 19 — artificialintelligenceact.eu/article/18/, /article/19/
counterEvidence.finding: Confirms verifier's source-precision point: retention duties (Art.18 10-yr tech docs; Art.19 ≥6-mo logs) flow from these articles, NOT Annex III, which only classifies. The cited source (Annex III) does not itself impose record-keeping.

final.statement: IF — and only if — the AI tutoring product is classified high-risk under AI Act Annex III §3, record-keeping obligations apply irrespective of user age across all EU/EEA states (DE, FR, SE, DK, EE, and NO via EEA incorporation). But two threshold defects must be resolved first: (1) Annex III §3 (a)-(d) all require an educational/vocational-training-institution context tied to admission, learning-outcome evaluation, level-assignment, or test-monitoring — a B2C consumer homework-helper with no grades/institution/admission role likely falls OUTSIDE §3, in which case it is not high-risk and no record-keeping attaches; (2) even if arguably in-scope, the Art. 6(3) filter can exempt a system performing a narrow procedural task that does not materially influence outcomes (though profiling of users would defeat that exemption). On sourcing: the retention duties are imposed by Articles 12/18/19 (logs ≥6 months; technical documentation 10 years) — Annex III §3 is only the classification anchor. On timing: as of 2026-06-05 the Digital Omnibus (political deal 7 May 2026) defers Annex III stand-alone high-risk compliance to 2 December 2027, binding only upon OJ publication (pending); Norway's AI Act is not yet in force (expected late summer 2026). The cell's unconditional present-tense 'applied... irrespective of age' framing is therefore premature both on the unestablished classification premise and on the deferred/not-yet-in-force timing.
final.reasoning: Verifier's age-neutrality, article-sourcing, and Omnibus-timing corrections are sound, but it skipped the threshold question: is a B2C consumer tutoring app even within Annex III §3? Points (a)-(d) all presuppose an educational-institution context plus admission/grading/monitoring functions — a homework-helper likely sits outside, defeating high-risk classification and the record-keeping duty. Art.6(3)'s narrow-task filter is a second off-ramp. Both the scope and the deferred/not-yet-in-force timing make the unconditional 'applied irrespective of age' framing overstated. originalClaim: ICO enforcement notices against Snap, BeReal for marketing-to-minors failures 2023-2024

final.statement: In 2023-24 the ICO did not issue finalized enforcement notices against Snap or BeReal. Snap got a PRELIMINARY enforcement notice (Oct 2023) over the My AI chatbot DPIA, closed May 2024 finding Snap compliant (no action). BeReal (with Sendit/Soda/X) was addressed via informal letters on geolocation and high-privacy defaults, prompting voluntary changes. The only hard enforcement was GBP 12.7M against TikTok (Apr 2023) for under-13 access without parental consent (Arts 8, 12, 5(1)(a)); the ICO expressly dropped the targeted-advertising/profiling finding from the final penalty. Marketing-to-minors / third-party data flows / data-retention framings are unsupported; actual themes were DPIA, geolocation, default privacy settings, age assurance, recommender systems.

final.statement: Slovakia: Act 18/2018 Coll. par. 15 sets digital consent at 16, no derogation - confirmed against the consolidated Slovak statute (slov-lex.sk, in force 2024-02-01). The matrix open cell is CLOSED.
